distractors.text: Replace the manual process immediately so benefits can be observed.
distractors.trap: Premature rollout ignores unresolved value concerns.

distractors.text: Ask the team to redesign the user interface for better acceptance.
distractors.trap: Assumes usability is the problem without evidence.

explanation: The project manager should first review the performance metrics with stakeholders to understand the specific concern and verify whether the system meets its agreed targets, before considering backlog changes or other actions.

eco_task: Integrate Project Planning Activities
prompts: A product team is using a prototyping approach to deliver a multiyear business initiative. The project manager notices that a few backlog items are taking longer than expected to be delivered. Which response is best?
On a long-running initiative using prototypes, several backlog items are moving through delivery more slowly than expected. What should the project manager do?
A team iterating on a business product sees some selected backlog work take longer than planned. Which action best supports improvement?
During an adaptive delivery effort, the project manager sees delays on several backlog items. What is the best way to address the issue?

correct.text: Conduct a retrospective with the team to identify bottlenecks.
correct.why: A retrospective lets the team inspect its process and improve flow.

correct.text: Facilitate a team retrospective focused on the delayed backlog items.
correct.why: The team can surface bottlenecks and agree on improvements.

correct.text: Use a retrospective to help the team find delivery constraints.
correct.why: Retrospectives support continuous improvement without premature escalation.

distractors.text: Review the master timeline and reallocate resources to delayed items.
distractors.trap: Resource movement may not address the cause.

distractors.text: Escalate the delays to senior management for immediate intervention.
distractors.trap: Premature before the team inspects its process.

distractors.text: Remove the delayed backlog items from the project scope.
distractors.trap: Cuts value before understanding the delay.

distractors.text: Ask the product owner to stop accepting new backlog items.
distractors.trap: Restricting backlog intake may not remove bottlenecks.

distractors.text: Assign delayed items to individual specialists for faster completion.
distractors.trap: Bypasses team-based problem solving.

distractors.text: Extend the next iteration to recover the delayed work.
distractors.trap: Changing timeboxes avoids process improvement.

explanation: The project manager should lead a retrospective so the team can identify the bottlenecks and threats to performance and suggest improvements, rather than reallocating resources, escalating, or cutting scope prematurely.

eco_task: Execute Project With the Urgency Required to Deliver Business Value
topic: Execute Project With the Urgency Required to Deliver Business Value
prompts: Halfway through developing a product, the organization acquires a similar product, potentially creating an overlap in the portfolio. What should the project manager do next?
Midway through a product initiative, the company buys another product that may duplicate the one being built. What is the project manager's best next action?
A project is developing a new platform when the organization acquires a comparable solution. The effort may no longer fit the portfolio. What should happen next?
During product development, an acquisition creates possible overlap with the in-progress product. How should the project manager respond first?

correct.text: Assess the project's feasibility and viability with the product owner.
correct.why: The product owner helps confirm whether the effort still aligns with value.

correct.text: Work with the product owner to reassess viability after the acquisition.
correct.why: Portfolio overlap requires confirming whether continued delivery remains justified.

correct.text: Review product fit and feasibility with the product owner.
correct.why: The product owner is the right partner to evaluate continued value.

distractors.text: Continue development because the product was already approved.
distractors.trap: Approval may be outdated after the acquisition.

distractors.text: Ask delivery specialists to analyze technical impact first.
distractors.trap: Technical impact follows the value alignment check.

distractors.text: Halt all work until executives publish a new roadmap.
distractors.trap: Stops prematurely before assessing viability.

distractors.text: Submit a change request to remove overlapping features immediately.
distractors.trap: Assumes the solution before confirming direction.

distractors.text: Transfer the team to integration work for the acquired product.
distractors.trap: Reassigns resources before a product decision.

distractors.text: Notify stakeholders that the current product will be cancelled.
distractors.trap: Cancellation is premature without viability review.

explanation: The project manager assesses the acquisition's impact with the product owner to align expectations before deciding the product's future; continuing or halting outright risks wasted effort.

eco_task: Plan and Manage Scope
prompts: A transportation company is transforming its operations and its core freight system. The scope statement exists and all stakeholders agreed on an iterative delivery approach. To help the team understand the work, what should the project manager do next?
A logistics firm has an approved scope statement for an iterative operations transformation. The team now needs clarity on the work to be performed. What should the project manager do?
Stakeholders have agreed on scope and an iterative approach for modernizing a freight platform. What is the next step to help the team understand the work?
For an operations modernization project, the scope statement has been approved and delivery will be iterative. How should the project manager help organize the work?

correct.text: Partner with the team to decompose the scope into a WBS and work packages.
correct.why: Work packages translate agreed scope into manageable planning components.

correct.text: Work with the team to break the approved scope into work packages.
correct.why: Decomposition helps the team understand and plan required outputs.

correct.text: Create the WBS with the team by decomposing the agreed scope.
correct.why: A WBS organizes scope into deliverable-based work packages.

distractors.text: Build the detailed schedule and assign specialists to each task.
distractors.trap: Scheduling and assignment follow decomposition.

distractors.text: Ask stakeholders to create timed tasks for each team member.
distractors.trap: Bypasses team planning and jumps to assignments.

distractors.text: Create a requirements management plan for assigning requirements.
distractors.trap: The issue is decomposing approved scope.

distractors.text: Begin sprint execution using the existing scope statement only.
distractors.trap: The team still needs work broken down.

distractors.text: Request additional funding before creating delivery work packages.
distractors.trap: Funding is not the stated constraint.

distractors.text: Have the sponsor prioritize the freight system deliverables.
distractors.trap: Prioritization does not replace scope decomposition.

explanation: To understand the work, the agreed scope must first be decomposed into a WBS with defined work packages; detailed scheduling and assignments depend on that decomposition.

eco_task: Execute Project With the Urgency Required to Deliver Business Value
topic: Execute Project With the Urgency Required to Deliver Business Value
prompts: A solution is being deployed across several countries. Country A requested and received a change to suit its market. When Country B received the new version, testing failed because it violated Country B's regulations. What upfront action would have avoided this?

A multinational rollout accepts a local change for one country. Later, another country's testing fails because the change conflicts with local regulation. What should have been done before implementation?
A regional team approves a market-specific change for one location, but the same release breaks compliance in another country. Which earlier action would have prevented this?
During a global deployment, one country's requested change is included in the common version and causes regulatory failure elsewhere. What should have happened first?

correct.text: Analyze every change request globally across all countries before implementing it.
correct.why: Global impact analysis would reveal conflicts with other countries' regulations.

correct.text: Perform cross-country impact analysis on each change before approval.
correct.why: Assessing all deployments prevents one market's change from harming another.

correct.text: Review proposed changes against all country requirements before release.
correct.why: A global review catches regulatory impacts before implementation.

distractors.text: Prevent any country-specific changes from being authorized.
distractors.trap: Blocks valid changes instead of managing impact.

distractors.text: Require all countries to adopt the first country's requirements.
distractors.trap: Ignores different regulations across markets.

distractors.text: Charge each requesting country for its own approved changes.
distractors.trap: Cost ownership does not address compliance impact.

distractors.text: Let each country test changes only after global deployment.
distractors.trap: Testing after deployment is too late.

distractors.text: Maintain separate unmanaged product versions for every country.
distractors.trap: Avoids governance and increases fragmentation.

distractors.text: Ask Country B to request an exception to its regulations.
distractors.trap: Regulatory conflicts must be prevented, not bypassed.

explanation: Because the same solution spans multiple countries, impact analysis of any change must consider all deployments, not just the requesting country; global analysis before implementation prevents such conflicts.

eco_task: Execute Project With the Urgency Required to Deliver Business Value
topic: Execute Project With the Urgency Required to Deliver Business Value
prompts: A business manager and product owner want a more flexible way to bring changes into the product backlog so they can cut time-to-market and respond to a competitor's release. What should the project manager do?
A product owner and business lead need to react quickly to a competitor by changing backlog priorities. What should the project manager do?
Market pressure requires faster incorporation of valuable product changes into the backlog. How should the project manager support the product owner?
A business team wants more flexibility in ordering backlog work so the product can reach the market faster. What is the best response?

correct.text: Coach the product owner to reprioritize the backlog in line with business goals.
correct.why: The product owner orders backlog work to maximize value and market response.

correct.text: Help the product owner reorder the backlog around current business priorities.
correct.why: Backlog prioritization is the agile mechanism for rapid value delivery.

correct.text: Support the product owner in continuously reprioritizing the backlog.
correct.why: Adaptive backlog ordering keeps delivery aligned with market changes.

distractors.text: Review the assumption log and project plan for possible changes.
distractors.trap: Analysis alone does not enable faster backlog response.

distractors.text: Create a formal change board for all product backlog changes.
distractors.trap: Heavy governance reduces needed flexibility.

distractors.text: Authorize all key stakeholders to change backlog items directly.
distractors.trap: Creates uncontrolled priorities and role confusion.

distractors.text: Freeze the backlog until the competitor's release is fully analyzed.
distractors.trap: Freezing work slows time-to-market.

distractors.text: Ask the team to select whichever backlog items are fastest.
distractors.trap: Speed alone may not align with business goals.

distractors.text: Escalate the competitor response to the change control board.
distractors.trap: Unnecessarily formal for adaptive backlog ordering.

explanation: A competitor's release can require a shift in direction, which the product owner addresses by reprioritizing the backlog to reflect business goals rather than adding heavy process.

eco_task: Plan and Manage Procurement
prompts: Reviewing status with the team, the project manager finds a vendor resource can be released early, confirms it against the schedule, and informs the vendor. The vendor insists the resource must stay for a set period. What should the project manager do?
A project manager determines a supplier's consultant is no longer needed according to the schedule, but the supplier says the person must remain for the contracted period. What is the next step?
After confirming a vendor resource can be released early, the vendor objects and cites a required minimum assignment period. How should the project manager respond?
The project schedule shows an external resource can be released, but the vendor disputes the release timing. What should the project manager do first?

correct.text: Review the terms and conditions in the vendor contract.
correct.why: Contract terms determine the parties' rights in a vendor disagreement.

correct.text: Check the contract to verify the agreed resource release terms.
correct.why: The agreement is the basis for resolving procurement disputes.

correct.text: Consult the vendor agreement before taking action on the release.
correct.why: Contractual obligations must be confirmed before changing vendor staffing.

distractors.text: Leave the schedule unchanged because the vendor says the resource must stay.
distractors.trap: Accepts the claim without checking the agreement.

distractors.text: Confirm the release and communicate the schedule change to stakeholders.
distractors.trap: Acts before resolving the contract dispute.

distractors.text: Release the resource immediately and document the vendor's objection.
distractors.trap: May breach terms that have not been verified.

distractors.text: Ask the team to find extra work for the vendor resource.
distractors.trap: Creates unnecessary work before checking obligations.

distractors.text: Escalate the vendor's position to the project sponsor.
distractors.trap: Escalation is premature without contract review.

distractors.text: Update the procurement management plan to allow early release.
distractors.trap: The contract, not the plan, governs the dispute.

explanation: Because this is a contractual disagreement, the project manager checks the vendor contract's terms and conditions to determine what is actually required before acting.

eco_task: Manage Project Issues
prompts: Following a recent incident, a dissatisfied stakeholder files a complaint about a project that has already been closed. What could the project manager do to help resolve it?
After formal closure, a stakeholder raises a complaint related to an incident on the completed project. How can the project manager help investigate?
A closed project receives a stakeholder complaint about prior project work. What should the project manager use to support resolution?
A business user complains about an issue tied to a project that has already ended. What is the best source for the project manager to review?

correct.text: Review the project management information system (PMIS) archives.
correct.why: Archived project records provide facts needed to investigate closed work.

correct.text: Use the PMIS archives to retrieve the closed project's records.
correct.why: The records preserve historical information after project closure.

correct.text: Check the archived project information in the PMIS.
correct.why: Closed-project issues should be investigated using retained project documentation.

distractors.text: Execute the stakeholder engagement plan for the closed project.
distractors.trap: The plan is not active after closure.

distractors.text: Meet with the sponsor to manage the dissatisfied stakeholder.
distractors.trap: Escalates before gathering documented facts.

distractors.text: Revise the closed project's risk register.
distractors.trap: Updating a closed register will not resolve the complaint.

distractors.text: Reopen the project so the team can address the incident.
distractors.trap: Reopening is not the first investigative step.

distractors.text: Ask former team members to recall what happened.
distractors.trap: Memory is less reliable than archived records.

distractors.text: Create a new issue log for the completed project.
distractors.trap: A new log does not provide historical evidence.

explanation: Because the project is closed, the project manager consults the PMIS archives to retrieve the records needed to investigate and address the complaint.

eco_task: Determine Appropriate Project Methodology/methods and Practices
topic: Determine Appropriate Project Methodology/methods and Practices
prompts: For an innovative project, the sponsor wants to build a large, creative data-processing platform, but its functionality cannot be clearly defined in the requirements. To secure continued funding, each deliverable showing progress will be demonstrated to management. Which approach should the project manager recommend?

A sponsor backs an innovative analytics platform, but the final functionality is not well understood. Management wants frequent demonstrations of completed deliverables before continuing funding. Which approach fits best?
A creative technology project has evolving requirements and needs regular demonstrations of working outputs to executives. What delivery approach should the project manager recommend?
A new data platform cannot be fully specified up front, and leadership wants visible progress through frequent deliverable demos. Which project approach is most appropriate?

correct.text: Agile
correct.why: Unclear requirements and frequent demonstrations fit iterative, incremental delivery.

correct.text: An agile approach
correct.why: Agile supports evolving scope through regular delivery and feedback.

correct.text: Agile delivery
correct.why: It enables visible progress when requirements cannot be fully defined upfront.

distractors.text: Predictive
distractors.trap: Predictive needs well-defined scope early.

distractors.text: Waterfall
distractors.trap: Waterfall is unsuitable when requirements remain unclear.

distractors.text: Hybrid
distractors.trap: Predictive elements add little when scope is highly uncertain.

distractors.text: Critical chain
distractors.trap: A scheduling method, not a fit for evolving requirements.

distractors.text: Phase-gate
distractors.trap: Stage approvals do not address unclear product functionality.

distractors.text: Traditional sequential delivery
distractors.trap: Sequential delivery depends on stable upfront requirements.

explanation: With unclear, evolving requirements and a need to show frequent progress for continued funding, agile's iterative deliveries and regular feedback fit best.

eco_task: Plan and Manage Quality of Products/deliverables
topic: Plan and Manage Quality of Products/deliverables
prompts: A team receives outsourced deliverables and determines they do not meet the agreed acceptance criteria. What should the project manager reference to verify this with the vendor?
A supplier submits deliverables that appear not to satisfy the agreed acceptance criteria. Which document should the project manager use with the supplier?
The project team reviews work from an external vendor and finds it does not match the accepted deliverable requirements. What should be referenced?
A vendor's completed work is disputed because it may not meet agreed acceptance criteria. What is the best reference for verification?

correct.text: Statement of work (SOW)
correct.why: The SOW defines vendor deliverables and acceptance expectations.

correct.text: The statement of work
correct.why: It specifies the work and criteria the vendor must satisfy.

correct.text: The vendor's SOW
correct.why: The SOW is the contractual reference for required deliverables.

distractors.text: Procurement management plan
distractors.trap: Describes procurement process, not detailed deliverable requirements.

distractors.text: Request for proposal (RFP)
distractors.trap: Used for solicitation, not acceptance verification.

distractors.text: Inspection checklist
distractors.trap: May record findings but does not define vendor scope.

distractors.text: Bid evaluation criteria
distractors.trap: Used to select vendors, not accept deliverables.

distractors.text: Source selection analysis
distractors.trap: Supports award decisions, not deliverable acceptance.

distractors.text: Procurement strategy
distractors.trap: Guides buying approach, not specific acceptance criteria.

explanation: The statement of work specifies all work needed for the deliverables to be completed and accepted, so it is the reference for verifying them against acceptance criteria with the vendor.

eco_task: Plan and Manage Project/phase Closure or Transitions
topic: Plan and Manage Project/phase Closure or Transitions
prompts: A new software program was delivered and put into production, and the project received administrative closure. Yet users keep contacting the project team for help and to report problems. What should the project manager have done to prevent this?
After a system goes live and the project is closed, users continue sending support requests to the former project team. What should have been arranged before closure?
A completed application is in production, but post-release issues still go to the project team after administrative closure. What should the project manager have ensured?
Following closure of a software implementation, the delivery team remains the informal help desk for users. Which earlier action would have prevented this?

correct.text: Ensure the product was transitioned to a group that supports it through its life cycle.
correct.why: Closure includes handing ongoing operations and support to the responsible group.

correct.text: Transition the production system to an operations support group before closure.
correct.why: Operations ownership prevents support requests from staying with the project team.

correct.text: Arrange formal handoff to the team responsible for ongoing product support.
correct.why: A support transition is required so users know where to go.

distractors.text: Include post-production support activities in the project scope.
distractors.trap: Keeps the project responsible instead of transitioning ownership.

distractors.text: Send a formal message announcing the project's closure.
distractors.trap: Communication alone does not establish support ownership.

distractors.text: Tell specialists to stop responding and direct users to documentation.
distractors.trap: Documentation does not replace an accountable support group.

distractors.text: Reopen the project until the number of support calls declines.
distractors.trap: Reopening avoids the missing transition.

distractors.text: Ask the sponsor to approve a larger project closure budget.
distractors.trap: Budget is not the root issue.

distractors.text: Create a new risk response plan for production incidents.
distractors.trap: The project is closed; operations support is needed.

explanation: At closure the deliverable should be handed to a group that operates, maintains, and supports it over its life cycle; without that transition, users keep contacting the disbanded project team.

eco_task: Determine Appropriate Project Methodology/methods and Practices
topic: Determine Appropriate Project Methodology/methods and Practices
prompts: A project manager is assigned to a new project where requirements are still evolving but business value must be delivered quickly. What approach should the project manager take?
A new initiative has changing requirements, but stakeholders need useful results as soon as possible. What should the project manager recommend?
The team is starting work before all requirements are stable, and the business expects early value. Which approach best fits?
A project faces evolving needs and pressure to deliver high-value outcomes quickly. What should the project manager do?

correct.text: Use a product backlog and complete the highest-priority requirements first.
correct.why: A prioritized backlog delivers value early while requirements continue to evolve.

correct.text: Manage work through a backlog ordered by business priority.
correct.why: Prioritized incremental delivery supports quick value under changing needs.

correct.text: Deliver the highest-value backlog items first using an adaptive approach.
correct.why: Adaptive backlog delivery balances evolving requirements with early business value.

distractors.text: Create risk mitigation strategies for all evolving requirements.
distractors.trap: Risk planning alone does not deliver value quickly.

distractors.text: Record every scope change in the requirements traceability matrix.
distractors.trap: Tracking changes does not prioritize delivery.

distractors.text: Create a feature burndown chart to report requirement progress.
distractors.trap: Reporting progress is not the delivery approach.

distractors.text: Wait until requirements are complete before authorizing development.
distractors.trap: Delays value when adaptive delivery is needed.

distractors.text: Ask the sponsor to freeze scope for the first release.
distractors.trap: Freezing scope conflicts with evolving needs.

distractors.text: Develop all low-risk requirements before high-value items.
distractors.trap: Risk alone should not override business value.

explanation: Evolving requirements with a need for early value call for an iterative approach: a product backlog delivering the highest-priority items first ensures value early and often.

eco_task: Plan and Manage Budget and Resources
topic: Plan and Manage Budget and Resources
prompts: In a cross-functional organization, a project manager must tell a department manager the number and type of resources needed and how long they will be needed. What should the project manager use to present this information?
A functional manager asks how many people of each skill type a project will need over time. Which tool should the project manager provide?
For resource negotiations, the project manager must show quantity, skill type, and duration of resource needs. What should be used?
A department head needs a clear view of project staffing demand by resource category across the schedule. Which artifact best presents this?

correct.text: Resource histogram
correct.why: A resource histogram shows resource quantities and types across time.

correct.text: A resource histogram
correct.why: It presents staffing demand by period, type, and amount.

correct.text: Resource usage histogram
correct.why: This view communicates how many resources are needed over time.

distractors.text: Resource assignments
distractors.trap: Assignments identify named resources after needs are planned.

distractors.text: Responsibility assignment matrix (RAM)
distractors.trap: Shows responsibilities, not quantity or duration.

distractors.text: Project organization chart
distractors.trap: Shows structure, not resource demand over time.

distractors.text: Team charter
distractors.trap: Defines team norms, not staffing quantities.

distractors.text: Stakeholder register
distractors.trap: Lists stakeholders, not project resource needs.

distractors.text: Resource calendar
distractors.trap: Shows availability, not the need profile itself.

explanation: A resource histogram displays resource needs over time by type and quantity, making it the tool to communicate how many resources are required and for how long.

eco_task: Evaluate and Deliver Project Benefits and Value
topic: Evaluate and Deliver Project Benefits and Value
prompts: In an agile development model, which approach should a project manager use to encourage collaboration among developers?
An agile software team wants stronger collaboration among its developers through a shared understanding of behavior. Which approach fits best?
A project manager wants developers on an agile team to collaborate using common language around expected system behavior. What should be used?
In an adaptive development environment, which technique best supports developer collaboration through shared examples and language?

correct.text: Behavior-driven development
correct.why: BDD uses shared language and behavior examples to improve collaboration.

correct.text: Behavior-driven development (BDD)
correct.why: BDD aligns developers around expected behavior using a common vocabulary.

correct.text: Use behavior-driven development
correct.why: It encourages collaboration through shared understanding of product behavior.

distractors.text: Acceptance testing
distractors.trap: Validates outcomes but is not primarily collaboration-focused.

distractors.text: Joint application development
distractors.trap: Focuses on user involvement in design sessions.

distractors.text: Rapid application development
distractors.trap: Emphasizes fast prototyping, not shared developer language.

distractors.text: Regression testing
distractors.trap: Checks existing behavior after changes.

distractors.text: Code inspection
distractors.trap: Reviews code quality, not collaborative behavior definition.

distractors.text: Test execution
distractors.trap: Runs tests rather than improving developer collaboration.

explanation: Behavior-driven development extends test-driven practices with a shared domain language, encouraging closer collaboration and common understanding among developers.

eco_task: Manage Project Issues
prompts: A project is estimated at 250 story points. The team planned 40 story points per iteration but delivered 40, 10, 20, and 15 in the first four iterations. Which action best addresses this?
An agile team expected to complete 40 story points each iteration, but its first four results were 40, 10, 20, and 15. What should the project manager do?
A 250-point effort was planned around 40 points per iteration. Actual completed points in the first four iterations are much lower overall. Which response is best?
After several iterations, a team's actual velocity is well below the planned 40 story points per iteration. How should the project manager respond?

correct.text: Work with the team to adjust planned story points for upcoming iterations.
correct.why: Future plans should reflect actual velocity rather than assumed capacity.

correct.text: Replan upcoming iterations with the team using observed velocity.
correct.why: Actual delivery data gives a more realistic basis for planning.

correct.text: Help the team revise iteration forecasts based on completed story points.
correct.why: Empirical velocity should guide commitments for later iterations.

distractors.text: Reassign undelivered tasks to team members for the next iteration.
distractors.trap: Agile teams self-organize; assignment misses velocity.

distractors.text: Remove undeliverable features from the backlog and notify the customer.
distractors.trap: Scope removal is premature.

distractors.text: Negotiate a deadline extension based on likely holiday delays.
distractors.trap: Does not address unrealistic velocity planning.

distractors.text: Increase the planned velocity to recover the missed story points.
distractors.trap: Raises expectations despite evidence of lower capacity.

distractors.text: Ask the team to work overtime until velocity reaches the plan.
distractors.trap: Treats capacity data as a motivation problem.

distractors.text: Replace low-performing team members before the next iteration.
distractors.trap: Blames individuals without using empirical planning.

explanation: Actual velocity (about 21 points) is well below the planned 40, so the team should collaboratively re-baseline the planned points to reflect real capacity.

eco_task: Evaluate and Deliver Project Benefits and Value
topic: Evaluate and Deliver Project Benefits and Value
prompts: During a business status meeting, the project manager notes that several market conditions have recently changed dramatically, and the in-progress project's expected value may now be threatened. What is the most appropriate next step?
A project manager learns that external market shifts may undermine the business value of an active project. What should the project manager do next?
At a portfolio update, new market information suggests the project's original value assumptions may no longer hold. What is the best response?
A project is still in progress when major market changes call its expected benefits into question. How should the project manager proceed?

correct.text: Meet with the executive sponsor to review the information and confirm continued support.
correct.why: The sponsor should reconfirm whether the project still merits delivery.

correct.text: Review the market changes with the sponsor and validate ongoing project value.
correct.why: Changed value assumptions require sponsor confirmation before proceeding.

correct.text: Engage the executive sponsor to reassess whether delivery remains justified.
correct.why: The sponsor owns the business case and value decision.

distractors.text: Stop the project immediately because expected value is uncertain.
distractors.trap: Stopping before sponsor review is premature.

distractors.text: Continue because no formal market-change notice was issued.
distractors.trap: Ignores relevant information affecting value.

distractors.text: Ask the team to research how the market changed over six months.
distractors.trap: Delays sponsor validation of value.

distractors.text: Update the risk register and keep executing the current plan.
distractors.trap: Risk tracking alone does not confirm value.

distractors.text: Request more budget to protect the original project benefits.
distractors.trap: Funding is not the first issue.

distractors.text: Tell stakeholders the project benefits are unchanged until closure.
distractors.trap: Dismisses a potential value shift.

explanation: Because the conditions underpinning the project's value have shifted, the PM should bring this to the executive sponsor to confirm continued organizational support rather than stopping or continuing unilaterally.

eco_task: Execute Project With the Urgency Required to Deliver Business Value
topic: Execute Project With the Urgency Required to Deliver Business Value
prompts: A demanding stakeholder insists an extra feature be added without changing scope, budget, or schedule. The project manager allows it because it adds great value, then discovers the feature has extended the schedule and raised costs. Which process should the project manager have followed before adding the feature?
A stakeholder requests a valuable new feature and says it should not affect the baseline. The project manager approves it, but cost and schedule increase. What process should have been used first?
A project manager accepts an added feature outside the agreed scope because it seems beneficial, then later finds budget and timeline impacts. Which process was missed?
An extra feature is added without formal evaluation, and the project later incurs additional cost and delay. What should the project manager have followed before approval?

correct.text: Change control
correct.why: Change control evaluates full impacts before approving scope additions.

correct.text: Integrated change control
correct.why: It reviews effects on scope, schedule, cost, and risk before approval.

correct.text: The formal change control process
correct.why: Formal review prevents unassessed features from altering baselines.

distractors.text: Schedule planning
distractors.trap: Addresses timing, not total change impact.

distractors.text: Procurement planning
distractors.trap: Concerns purchasing, not feature approval.

distractors.text: Quality audit
distractors.trap: Reviews process compliance, not change impact.

distractors.text: Scope validation
distractors.trap: Accepts completed deliverables, not proposed changes.

distractors.text: Cost estimating
distractors.trap: Only one part of change evaluation.

distractors.text: Stakeholder engagement planning
distractors.trap: Does not authorize or assess scope changes.

explanation: Adding any feature is a change that must go through change control, which assesses the full impact on scope, budget, schedule, and risk before it is approved and integrated.

eco_task: Evaluate and Deliver Project Benefits and Value
topic: Evaluate and Deliver Project Benefits and Value
prompts: A project to create a new product is completed and launched, but sales are far below expectations. Analysis shows the product is misaligned with the organization's core competencies. During which activity should this have been discovered?

A healthcare firm launches a new service after the project finishes, but adoption is much lower than expected. A review finds the offering did not fit the organization's strengths. Where should this mismatch have been identified?

A technology company releases a completed product, then learns the market response is weak because the product does not align with its core capabilities. Which activity should have uncovered this earlier?

After a public-sector project delivers a new solution, expected benefits do not materialize. The root cause is that the solution was not aligned with the agency's strategic competencies. When should that have been discovered?

correct.text: Reviewing the business case
correct.why: The business case confirms strategic fit and organizational capability before committing investment.

correct.text: Validating the business case
correct.why: Business case validation checks whether the proposed product supports strategy and competencies.

correct.text: During the business case review
correct.why: Strategic alignment and expected value should be tested in the business case.

distractors.text: Performing what-if scenario analysis
distractors.trap: Scenario analysis tests alternatives, not core strategic fit.

distractors.text: Conducting strategic performance evaluation
distractors.trap: Performance evaluation happens after outcomes begin appearing.

distractors.text: Defining the project scope
distractors.trap: Scope defines deliverables, not organizational suitability.

distractors.text: Preparing the acceptance criteria
distractors.trap: Acceptance criteria judge outputs, not business justification.

distractors.text: Completing benefits measurement
distractors.trap: Benefits measurement occurs after delivery, too late.

distractors.text: Updating the product roadmap
distractors.trap: Roadmaps sequence work; they do not validate investment fit.

explanation: The business case review establishes whether a product aligns with the organization's core competencies and strategy, so the misalignment should have surfaced there.

eco_task: Execute Project With the Urgency Required to Deliver Business Value
topic: Execute Project With the Urgency Required to Deliver Business Value
prompts: During a Scrum board review on a hybrid project, a team member suggests adding a task that is outside the baseline scope but could reduce project risk. Which response is best?
In a hybrid delivery project, a developer proposes a new task during board review. It is beyond the approved baseline, but it may lower an important risk. What should the project manager do?
While reviewing sprint work for a hybrid initiative, the team identifies an extra activity not in the baseline that could reduce uncertainty. What is the best response?
On a hybrid software project, a team member recommends adding work outside the current scope baseline because it could mitigate a project risk. How should this be handled?

correct.text: Assess the task's feasibility and impact, then add it to a future sprint if suitable.
correct.why: Evaluate impact first, then incorporate valuable work through appropriate agile planning.

correct.text: Evaluate the impact and consider the task for a later sprint if it is justified.
correct.why: Hybrid delivery still requires impact analysis before adapting planned work.

correct.text: Analyze the proposed task and schedule it in an upcoming sprint if beneficial.
correct.why: Risk-reducing work should be assessed before being added to future iteration plans.

distractors.text: Ask the product owner to compare it only against the original requirements.
distractors.trap: This ignores risk value and narrows evaluation too much.

distractors.text: Tell the team to stay on the critical path and defer discussion.
distractors.trap: Deferral avoids assessing the potential risk benefit.

distractors.text: Document it as out of scope and take no further action.
distractors.trap: Recording alone misses a possible mitigation opportunity.

distractors.text: Add it immediately to the current sprint backlog.
distractors.trap: Immediate insertion disrupts current commitments without analysis.

distractors.text: Reject it because it is outside the baseline scope.
distractors.trap: Hybrid approaches still allow evaluated change.

distractors.text: Escalate the idea directly to the sponsor for approval.
distractors.trap: Escalation skips team-level impact analysis.

explanation: The PM should analyze the task's feasibility and impact and, if beneficial, incorporate it into future sprints through the appropriate change process, rather than deferring or merely documenting it.

eco_task: Integrate Project Planning Activities
prompts: While reviewing progress mid-iteration, the agile coach points out a major risk arising from a user story dependency. What is the most appropriate next step?
During an iteration review, a Scrum master notices a significant risk tied to a dependency between two backlog items. What should the project manager do next?
Midway through a sprint, the team identifies that one user story depends on another in a way that could threaten delivery. What is the best immediate action?
An agile coach flags a newly discovered dependency risk while the team is checking current iteration progress. Which action should come first?

correct.text: Update the risk log.
correct.why: A newly identified risk should be captured before responses are planned.

correct.text: Record the risk in the risk register.
correct.why: New risks must be documented so they can be analyzed and managed.

correct.text: Add the dependency risk to the risk log.
correct.why: The risk log is the proper place to capture newly identified risks.

distractors.text: Update the product backlog.
distractors.trap: Backlog changes do not formally capture the risk.

distractors.text: Update the project charter.
distractors.trap: The charter is not used for individual risks.

distractors.text: Update the issue tracker.
distractors.trap: A risk has not yet become an issue.

distractors.text: Create a change request immediately.
distractors.trap: Impact and response are not assessed yet.

distractors.text: Revise the sprint goal.
distractors.trap: Changing goals is premature before logging the risk.

distractors.text: Escalate the dependency to the sponsor.
distractors.trap: Escalation comes after analysis, if needed.

explanation: On learning of a new major risk, the PM should first record it in the risk log before touching the backlog, charter, or issue tracker.

eco_task: Plan and Manage Quality of Products/deliverables
topic: Plan and Manage Quality of Products/deliverables
prompts: A project is being audited to ensure compliance with organizational policies and processes. Which process is being conducted?
An internal audit team reviews whether a project is following the company's approved procedures and process standards. Which process does this represent?
A project manager is asked to support an audit focused on process compliance, policy adherence, and use of organizational standards. Which project management process is underway?
A compliance group examines a project to confirm the team is applying required organizational methods correctly. What process is being performed?

correct.text: Manage Quality
correct.why: Quality audits that check process compliance are part of Manage Quality.

correct.text: The Manage Quality process
correct.why: Manage Quality includes auditing whether approved processes and policies are followed.

correct.text: Performing Manage Quality
correct.why: Process-oriented quality audits belong to Manage Quality, not deliverable inspection.

distractors.text: Audit Schedule
distractors.trap: This is not a recognized project management process.

distractors.text: Plan Quality Management
distractors.trap: Planning defines standards; it does not audit compliance.

distractors.text: Control Quality
distractors.trap: Control Quality inspects deliverables, not processes.

distractors.text: Monitor Risks
distractors.trap: Risk monitoring does not perform quality audits.

distractors.text: Validate Scope
distractors.trap: Scope validation accepts deliverables, not processes.

distractors.text: Direct and Manage Project Work
distractors.trap: This executes work but does not define audit activity.

explanation: Auditing for compliance with organizational policies and processes is a quality audit, an activity of the Manage Quality process, not Plan Quality Management or Control Quality.

eco_task: Execute Project With the Urgency Required to Deliver Business Value
topic: Execute Project With the Urgency Required to Deliver Business Value
prompts: You are modernizing a bank's IT infrastructure and recently had the change management plan approved by the executive sponsor. The sponsor now requests that you implement a new piece of technology in the solution. What is the most appropriate next step?

A project manager has just received approval for the change management plan on a financial-services infrastructure project. The sponsor then asks to add a new technology component. What should happen next?
During an IT modernization effort, the executive sponsor requests adding a new technical capability after the change process has been approved. How should the project manager proceed?
On a bank platform upgrade, the sponsor asks the team to include a newly available technology in the solution. The change management plan is already approved. What is the next step?

correct.text: Submit a change request to the governance review board.
correct.why: A scope change must be evaluated and approved through formal change control.

correct.text: Route the request through the approved change control process.
correct.why: Sponsor requests still require formal assessment before implementation.

correct.text: Prepare a formal change request for governance review.
correct.why: The new technology changes the solution and needs governance approval.

distractors.text: Add the new technology as a risk in the risk log.
distractors.trap: A requested scope change is not simply a risk.

distractors.text: Update the schedule and cost baselines immediately.
distractors.trap: Baselines change only after approval.

distractors.text: Ask the sponsor to procure the technology.
distractors.trap: Procurement before approval bypasses change control.

distractors.text: Direct the team to implement the technology.
distractors.trap: Implementation before evaluation violates the change plan.

distractors.text: Record the request as a lesson learned.
distractors.trap: Lessons learned do not authorize solution changes.

distractors.text: Update the project charter with the technology.
distractors.trap: The charter is not updated to approve detailed changes.

explanation: Even at the sponsor's request, a change to scope should be documented and submitted as a change request for evaluation and approval before baselines or procurement are touched.

eco_task: Execute Project With the Urgency Required to Deliver Business Value
topic: Execute Project With the Urgency Required to Deliver Business Value
prompts: During a project, the team realizes recent data center upgrades could affect the outcome because certain modules of the deliverable are incompatible with the new environment. The team has identified the appropriate steps to resolve the issue. What is the project manager's next step?
A team discovers that platform upgrades in the client's data center make several deliverable modules incompatible. The corrective steps are known. What should the project manager do next?
During execution, new infrastructure changes create compatibility problems with parts of the planned deliverable. The team has determined how to fix them. What is the next project management action?
A project deliverable no longer works with the updated hosting environment. The team has identified the work needed to resolve the incompatibility. What should the project manager do?

correct.text: Submit a change request.
correct.why: Changes affecting the deliverable must be handled through change control.

correct.text: Raise a formal change request.
correct.why: The proposed fix changes project work and requires approval.

correct.text: Initiate the change control process.
correct.why: Deliverable-impacting changes need formal review before implementation.

distractors.text: Request a budget increase.
distractors.trap: Budget changes follow approval, not precede it.

distractors.text: Revise the project schedule.
distractors.trap: The schedule should not change before approval.

distractors.text: Update the requirements document.
distractors.trap: Requirements updates follow an approved change.

distractors.text: Tell the team to implement the fix.
distractors.trap: Implementation skips formal change control.

distractors.text: Close the issue as resolved.
distractors.trap: The resolution has not been approved or applied.

distractors.text: Ask operations to reverse the upgrades.
distractors.trap: This assumes control outside the project.

explanation: Because resolving the incompatibility alters the deliverable, the PM should submit a change request before adjusting budget, schedule, or requirements.

eco_task: Evaluate and Deliver Project Benefits and Value
topic: Evaluate and Deliver Project Benefits and Value
prompts: The business environment has become unstable because a stakeholder altered the project's outputs and outcomes mid-execution. Considering this stakeholder, how should the project manager ensure the project benefits are still delivered?
Midway through delivery, a high-impact stakeholder changes expectations for the project's outputs and outcomes, creating uncertainty around benefits. How should the project manager respond?
A stakeholder's requested shift in project outcomes has made the business environment less stable during execution. What should the project manager do to protect expected benefits?
During execution, a stakeholder changes the desired outputs and outcomes of a project. How should the project manager manage this stakeholder so benefits can still be achieved?

correct.text: Engage the stakeholder by impact level and capture needs through change control.
correct.why: Impact-based engagement and change control keep benefits aligned with revised needs.

correct.text: Manage the stakeholder according to impact and process the needs as changes.
correct.why: Stakeholder influence should guide engagement while changes are formally evaluated.

correct.text: Tailor engagement to the stakeholder's impact and use the change process.
correct.why: This balances stakeholder management with controlled benefit alignment.

distractors.text: Confirm team agreement and send the stakeholder frequent updates.
distractors.trap: Communication alone does not manage changed outcomes.

distractors.text: Switch to a hybrid method to minimize scope deviation.
distractors.trap: Changing method does not address the stakeholder's needs.

distractors.text: Keep outputs aligned only to the original requirements.
distractors.trap: Ignoring legitimate changes may lose benefits.

distractors.text: Escalate the stakeholder's behavior to senior leadership.
distractors.trap: Escalation is premature before engagement and change control.

distractors.text: Freeze the scope until the environment stabilizes.
distractors.trap: Freezing scope may block needed benefit alignment.

distractors.text: Ask the team to absorb the requested changes informally.
distractors.trap: Informal absorption bypasses control and transparency.

explanation: The PM should engage the stakeholder according to their impact and route their changed needs through the change management process to keep benefits on track.

eco_task: Execute Project With the Urgency Required to Deliver Business Value
topic: Execute Project With the Urgency Required to Deliver Business Value
prompts: A customer's requested design change is approved by the governance review board. The project manager then discovers the change will cause a three-month delay. What should the project manager have done to avoid this?
After a client design change receives governance approval, the project manager learns it will significantly delay the schedule. What should have happened before approval?
A governance board approves a requested design update, but only afterward does the team discover a major schedule delay. Which earlier action would have prevented this surprise?
A customer's design modification is accepted through governance review. Later, the project manager finds it will push delivery back by three months. What should have been done first?

correct.text: Thoroughly assess the request using the risk management plan.
correct.why: Preapproval impact and risk assessment would have revealed the schedule delay.

correct.text: Evaluate the change's risks and impacts before governance approval.
correct.why: The board needs schedule impact information before deciding.

correct.text: Analyze the request according to the risk management approach.
correct.why: Proper risk analysis identifies consequences before a change is approved.

distractors.text: Increase the budget and request more resources.
distractors.trap: Extra resources do not replace impact analysis.

distractors.text: Redefine the scope to include the new design.
distractors.trap: Scope updates without analysis hide schedule impact.

distractors.text: Fast track and crash the schedule.
distractors.trap: Compression reacts to delay after discovery.

distractors.text: Ask the customer to withdraw the request.
distractors.trap: Withdrawal avoids evaluation rather than improving it.

distractors.text: Update the lessons learned register.
distractors.trap: Lessons learned do not prevent the approval error.

distractors.text: Reassign the project scheduler.
distractors.trap: Staff changes do not address missing assessment.

explanation: Assessing the change against the risk management plan before approval would have revealed the three-month delay, allowing an informed decision.

eco_task: Execute Project With the Urgency Required to Deliver Business Value
topic: Execute Project With the Urgency Required to Deliver Business Value
prompts: You manage an agile project in its third iteration. During the current iteration, the customer requests additional important features that could be accommodated without significant extra resources and asks that they be added to the current iteration immediately. How should you respond?

In the middle of an agile iteration, a customer asks for valuable new features that do not appear to require much extra capacity. The customer wants them included right away. What should the project manager do?
During the current sprint, a client requests additional high-priority functionality and says the team should add it now because the effort seems small. How should the request be handled?

An agile team is already working through an iteration when the customer identifies important new features. They can likely fit within existing resources, but the customer wants immediate inclusion. What is the best response?

correct.text: Prioritize the features for the start of the next iteration.
correct.why: This protects current commitments while honoring customer collaboration.

correct.text: Place the features for prioritization in the next sprint planning.
correct.why: New work should be considered at the next planning point.

correct.text: Queue the features for the next iteration's prioritization.
correct.why: Deferring to the next iteration avoids disrupting committed sprint work.

distractors.text: Remind the customer that contract changes are not allowed.
distractors.trap: Agile delivery should not reject change outright.

distractors.text: Add the features to the current iteration immediately.
distractors.trap: Mid-iteration insertion disrupts current commitments.

distractors.text: Promise the features will be added at a later stage.
distractors.trap: Vague timing does not support transparent planning.

distractors.text: Ask the sponsor to decide whether the team should add them.
distractors.trap: This bypasses normal backlog prioritization.

distractors.text: Extend the current iteration to include the features.
distractors.trap: Changing iteration length undermines cadence.

distractors.text: Reject the request until the release is complete.
distractors.trap: This ignores agile responsiveness to value.

explanation: The features should be acknowledged and prioritized for the next iteration, letting the team finish current work without disruption while still responding to the customer.

eco_task: Plan and Manage Quality of Products/deliverables
topic: Plan and Manage Quality of Products/deliverables
prompts: A developer has completed a feature, but the product owner is unsure how to assess the quality of the output. Given that the project uses an agile approach, which tool or technique should the product owner use?
On an agile product team, a completed feature is ready for product owner feedback. The product owner needs an appropriate way to evaluate the output's quality. Which technique fits best?
A product owner wants to assess whether a delivered agile feature meets expectations, but is unsure which approach to use. What tool or technique is most appropriate?
After a developer finishes a user-facing feature, the product owner needs to inspect and provide feedback in an agile setting. Which technique should be used?

correct.text: Use product demonstrations and reviews of the completed feature
correct.why: Agile demos and reviews provide visibility and feedback on completed work.

correct.text: Assess the feature through agile demonstrations or reviews
correct.why: These agile feedback points help assess whether the output meets expectations.

correct.text: Inspect the completed increment during sprint demos and reviews
correct.why: Reviewing demonstrated increments is a standard agile quality feedback technique.

distractors.text: Rely on expert judgment to decide if the feature is acceptable
distractors.trap: Too generic for agile product feedback.

distractors.text: Use a formal decision-making technique to choose acceptance
distractors.trap: Decision making is broad, not quality assessment.

distractors.text: Complete a checklist without product owner review feedback
distractors.trap: Checklists alone miss agile stakeholder feedback.

distractors.text: Create an affinity diagram to group feedback themes
distractors.trap: Affinity diagrams organize ideas, not inspect increments.

distractors.text: Perform a cost-benefit analysis of the delivered feature
distractors.trap: Evaluates economics, not feature quality.

distractors.text: Optimize team resources before evaluating the feature output
distractors.trap: Resource optimization addresses capacity, not quality.

explanation: Demonstrations and reviews are the agile mechanism for the product owner to inspect a completed feature and provide feedback, unlike the predictive-oriented alternatives.

eco_task: Evaluate and Deliver Project Benefits and Value
topic: Evaluate and Deliver Project Benefits and Value
prompts: You have started developing the plan for a new project. In the first planning meeting, stakeholders ask about the project's value and how it relates to the organization's goals. What is the most appropriate next step?
At the first planning session for a new initiative, stakeholders want to understand why the project matters and how it supports business objectives. Where should the project manager direct them?
While planning begins, several stakeholders ask how the project is expected to create value and align with strategic goals. What should the project manager do?
Early in project planning, stakeholders question the project's business value and connection to organizational strategy. What is the best response?

correct.text: Refer the stakeholders to the project charter.
correct.why: The charter documents project purpose, value, and strategic alignment.

correct.text: Use the project charter to answer their questions.
correct.why: The charter contains the high-level value and organizational goal alignment.

correct.text: Point them to the approved project charter.
correct.why: Project justification and strategic fit are captured in the charter.

distractors.text: Refer them to the scope management plan.
distractors.trap: This plan may not exist yet and focuses on scope.

distractors.text: Invite the executive sponsor to answer instead.
distractors.trap: The documented answer should already be in the charter.

distractors.text: Ask the portfolio office for documentation.
distractors.trap: The PM should use project-specific documentation.

distractors.text: Create the work breakdown structure.
distractors.trap: A WBS does not explain strategic value.

distractors.text: Review the requirements traceability matrix.
distractors.trap: Requirements traceability is too detailed for this need.

distractors.text: Update the communications management plan.
distractors.trap: Communication planning does not define project value.

explanation: The project charter captures the project's value and alignment with organizational goals, making it the right reference for the stakeholders' questions.

eco_task: Plan and Manage Quality of Products/deliverables
topic: Plan and Manage Quality of Products/deliverables
prompts: A company is using the Lean Six Sigma methodology on a project. Midway through, an external Lean consultant is brought in to audit the project and confirm the methodology is being applied correctly. Which process does this audit represent?
A project team is following Lean Six Sigma. Halfway through delivery, a consultant reviews whether the methodology is being used as intended. Which process is represented?
An organization brings in an outside specialist to audit a project team's use of an approved process methodology. What project management process is this?
Mid-project, an external advisor checks whether a team is correctly applying the company's chosen improvement methodology. Which process is being conducted?

correct.text: Manage Quality
correct.why: Auditing process and standard adherence is a Manage Quality activity.

correct.text: The Manage Quality process
correct.why: Manage Quality includes quality audits of methods, processes, and standards.

correct.text: Performing Manage Quality
correct.why: This audit verifies process use rather than inspecting final deliverables.

distractors.text: Control Quality
distractors.trap: Control Quality inspects outputs, not methodology use.

distractors.text: Perform Qualitative Risk Analysis
distractors.trap: Risk prioritization is not a quality audit.

distractors.text: Plan Quality Management
distractors.trap: Planning defines quality standards before execution.

distractors.text: Validate Scope
distractors.trap: Validate Scope formalizes acceptance of deliverables.

distractors.text: Monitor Stakeholder Engagement
distractors.trap: Stakeholder engagement does not audit methodology compliance.

distractors.text: Control Procurements
distractors.trap: Procurement control manages supplier agreements, not methods.

explanation: A quality audit that verifies correct use of processes and standards is part of Manage Quality; Control Quality instead inspects the deliverables against those standards.

eco_task: Execute Project With the Urgency Required to Deliver Business Value
topic: Execute Project With the Urgency Required to Deliver Business Value
prompts: During execution, a customer submits a change request to protect the project's business value against a new government regulation. The project manager's analysis shows the change requires reworking over half the project output and adds cost for the customer. What should the project manager do?
A new regulation leads a customer to request a change during execution. Analysis shows the change would require major rework and additional customer cost. What should the project manager do next?
During project work, a client asks for a change to preserve business value under a newly issued rule. The PM finds the change has substantial rework and cost impacts. How should it be handled?
A customer proposes a regulatory change that would affect much of the delivered output and increase costs. The impact has been analyzed. What is the appropriate action?

correct.text: Submit the request to the CCB with the reason and cost-impact analysis.
correct.why: The CCB needs impact information to make an informed decision.

correct.text: Send the change request and cost impact to the change control board.
correct.why: Significant rework and cost impacts require formal board review.

correct.text: Present the request to the CCB with its business reason and impact.
correct.why: Formal review should include why the change is needed and its effects.

distractors.text: Submit it noting the customer will absorb all extra costs.
distractors.trap: Payment responsibility does not replace impact review.

distractors.text: Advise the CCB to reject it because of the impact.
distractors.trap: The PM should present facts, not bias the decision.

distractors.text: Show no impact and proceed without notifying the CCB.
distractors.trap: This conceals material information and bypasses control.

distractors.text: Start the rework because regulation compliance is mandatory.
distractors.trap: Urgency does not remove change control.

distractors.text: Update the baseline to include the new regulation.
distractors.trap: Baselines change only after approval.

distractors.text: Ask the customer to approve the added cost directly.
distractors.trap: Customer approval alone is not integrated change control.

explanation: A change request should document the reason and full impact for CCB review; with a major cost impact, the cost-impact analysis must be included and no material information may be omitted.

eco_task: Manage Project Issues
prompts: After the kick-off meeting, a key resource tells the project manager they may not be able to deliver their assigned tasks because of competing priorities. What is the best response?
Soon after kickoff, a critical team member says other assignments may prevent them from completing their project work. How should the project manager respond?
A key specialist informs the project manager that conflicting priorities could keep them from meeting assigned commitments. What is the best next step?
Following project kickoff, an important resource reports that workload conflicts may affect their ability to deliver assigned tasks. What should the PM do?

correct.text: Schedule a team meeting to assess and manage priorities.
correct.why: A team discussion surfaces commitments so priorities and capacity can be managed.

correct.text: Hold a team meeting to review commitments and priorities.
correct.why: The team can align workload and address the availability concern collaboratively.

correct.text: Bring the team together to assess competing priorities.
correct.why: Shared visibility helps resolve capacity conflicts before they become delays.

distractors.text: Document the availability concern in the risk log.
distractors.trap: Logging alone does not resolve the priority conflict.

distractors.text: Escalate the issue to the executive sponsor.
distractors.trap: Escalation is premature before team-level resolution.

distractors.text: Update the project management plan for a delay.
distractors.trap: This accepts delay before addressing the cause.

distractors.text: Reassign the resource's work to another team member.
distractors.trap: Reassignment is premature without assessing priorities.

distractors.text: Ask the resource to work overtime.
distractors.trap: Overtime ignores root causes and team capacity.

distractors.text: Remove the tasks from the project scope.
distractors.trap: Scope removal is disproportionate and uncontrolled.

explanation: Convening the team to assess and manage priorities addresses the root cause, allowing capacity leveling and support for the key resource, rather than merely logging a risk, escalating, or planning for delay.

eco_task: Plan and Manage Schedule
prompts: During a schedule review, the team reports that a critical-path activity has slipped because of an external dependency, putting the schedule at risk. What should the project manager do first to address the issue?
A project team finds that an outside dependency has delayed a critical-path task and may affect the overall timeline. What should the project manager do first?
In a schedule status meeting, the team identifies slippage on a critical-path activity caused by an external party. What is the first appropriate response?
A critical-path work package is behind schedule due to an external dependency. Before communicating broader impacts, what should the project manager do first?

correct.text: Update the documentation and identify corrective actions.
correct.why: Current documentation supports analysis and coordinated corrective action.

correct.text: Document the issue and determine corrective actions.
correct.why: Recording the slip first enables informed analysis and response planning.

correct.text: Update project records and analyze corrective options.
correct.why: Accurate records are needed before deciding or communicating next steps.

distractors.text: Issue a change request and meet with the customer.
distractors.trap: Change requests follow analysis, not precede it.

distractors.text: Rebaseline the schedule and inform the sponsor.
distractors.trap: Rebaselining is premature before assessment.

distractors.text: Ask the customer to extend the timeline.
distractors.trap: Customer requests come after internal analysis.

distractors.text: Crash the critical-path activity immediately.
distractors.trap: Compression should be evaluated before use.

distractors.text: Escalate the dependency to the governance board.
distractors.trap: Escalation may be needed later, after analysis.

distractors.text: Remove the activity from the schedule baseline.
distractors.trap: Removing baseline work is not an initial response.

explanation: The first step is to update project documentation and identify corrective actions, enabling proper analysis; customer communication or baseline changes should follow only after the issue is assessed internally.

eco_task: Plan and Manage Project/phase Closure or Transitions
topic: Plan and Manage Project/phase Closure or Transitions
prompts: As the project closes, the team argues that the final sprint retrospective is unnecessary because no sprints remain. What is a potentially beneficial outcome of holding this retrospective anyway?
At project closeout, an agile team questions whether a final retrospective has value since the work is ending. What benefit can it still provide?
The last iteration has finished, and team members say a retrospective is not useful because no further sprint is planned. Why should the project manager still hold it?
During closure, the delivery team wants to skip the final retrospective because the project is complete. What outcome could make the session worthwhile?

correct.text: Insights and lessons learned that could benefit future projects.
correct.why: A final retrospective captures improvement knowledge for similar future work.

correct.text: Lessons learned that can improve future initiatives.
correct.why: Closure retrospectives preserve knowledge beyond the current project.

correct.text: Actionable learning for future teams and projects.
correct.why: The session identifies practices to repeat or improve later.

distractors.text: Improved velocity if the same team is assembled later.
distractors.trap: Future identical team composition is speculative.

distractors.text: Promotion opportunities for delivery specialists.
distractors.trap: Career advancement is not the purpose.

distractors.text: Tips for adjusting if these members work together again.
distractors.trap: Team familiarity is narrower than project learning.

distractors.text: Formal acceptance of all completed deliverables.
distractors.trap: Acceptance is handled through closure and validation.

distractors.text: Approval to start another sprint immediately.
distractors.trap: The project is closing, not extending.

distractors.text: A replacement for the final project report.
distractors.trap: Retrospectives do not replace closure documentation.

explanation: Even with no sprints remaining, a final retrospective lets the team reflect, identify improvements, and capture knowledge that can be applied to future projects and continuous improvement.

eco_task: Manage Project Issues
prompts: Some project materials were ordered from overseas. The supplier reports the materials will arrive late due to a customs clearance delay that affects the critical path. Which action should come first?
A supplier tells the project manager that imported materials will be delayed in customs, and the delay affects critical-path work. What should the project manager do first?
Project components sourced internationally are now delayed because of customs clearance. The affected work is on the critical path. Which action comes first?
An overseas shipment needed for critical-path activities will arrive late due to customs processing. What is the first response the PM should take?

correct.text: Review the risk log and carry out the associated risk response.
correct.why: A planned response should be enacted when the identified risk occurs.

correct.text: Check the risk register and implement the planned response.
correct.why: The risk response plan guides action for realized risks.

correct.text: Use the documented risk response from the risk log.
correct.why: Known risks should be handled according to their planned responses.

distractors.text: Ask logistics to arrange expedited customs clearance.
distractors.trap: Customs clearance may not be controllable.

distractors.text: Add the root cause to the bid documents.
distractors.trap: Bid documents are no longer relevant.

distractors.text: Require the supplier to cover added costs.
distractors.trap: Cost liability depends on contract terms.

distractors.text: Rebaseline the schedule for the delay.
distractors.trap: Rebaselining is premature before response execution.

distractors.text: Notify the sponsor that delivery will slip.
distractors.trap: Communication should follow planned response review.

distractors.text: Cancel the order and find a new supplier.
distractors.trap: Supplier replacement is drastic without reviewing responses.

explanation: Because the delay was anticipated as a risk, the project manager should first consult the risk log and execute the planned response rather than pursuing expediting, root-cause analysis, or cost recovery.

eco_task: Execute Project With the Urgency Required to Deliver Business Value
topic: Execute Project With the Urgency Required to Deliver Business Value
prompts: During implementation, an issue arises that requires executing the project's contingency plan, which will affect the project schedule. What is the best response?
A realized issue triggers the project's contingency plan, and using that plan will change the schedule. What should the project manager do?
During execution, the team must activate a contingency plan. The planned response will affect the approved timeline. What is the best next action?
An issue occurs and the approved contingency response needs to be used, but it has schedule implications. How should the project manager proceed?

correct.text: Issue a change request.
correct.why: The schedule impact must be formally documented and controlled.

correct.text: Submit a formal change request.
correct.why: Using the contingency plan changes the schedule and needs change control.

correct.text: Raise a change request for the schedule impact.
correct.why: Formal approval keeps stakeholders aligned on the timeline change.

distractors.text: Initiate a risk audit.
distractors.trap: An audit does not manage the current schedule impact.

distractors.text: Review only the cost impact.
distractors.trap: Cost review alone misses the schedule change.

distractors.text: Update the contingency plan.
distractors.trap: Plan updates follow approved change decisions.

distractors.text: Implement the plan without notifying stakeholders.
distractors.trap: Schedule impacts require visibility and control.

distractors.text: Close the issue after activating the response.
distractors.trap: Closure is premature while impacts remain unmanaged.

distractors.text: Ask the sponsor to absorb the delay.
distractors.trap: Sponsor awareness does not replace change control.

explanation: Executing a contingency plan that affects the schedule is a change to the baseline, so issuing a change request is the first step to formally document and manage it; cost review and plan updates follow.

eco_task: Determine Appropriate Project Methodology/methods and Practices
topic: Determine Appropriate Project Methodology/methods and Practices
prompts: A service provider and client agree on most of the scope of a marketing-content contract. The client has a fixed budget and target date and wants maximum business value by adding scope on a best-effort basis near the end. What is the best response?

A client and vendor have defined most work for a content campaign, but the client wants flexibility to add lower-priority scope near completion while keeping cost and date fixed. What contract approach fits best?

A creative services contract has a fixed budget and deadline, with most scope already agreed. The client wants remaining capacity used for the highest-value extra work late in the effort. What should be proposed?
A customer wants a marketing deliverables agreement that protects a fixed date and budget while allowing best-effort scope additions near the end. Which response is best?

correct.text: Propose a dynamic-scope contract with a fixed number of iterations.
correct.why: Fixed iterations allow prioritized best-effort scope within fixed cost and time.

correct.text: Use dynamic scope with fixed iterations.
correct.why: This preserves budget and date while flexing lower-priority scope.

correct.text: Recommend fixed iterations with dynamically prioritized scope.
correct.why: Iteration limits control cost and schedule while maximizing value.

distractors.text: Base the contract on agreed scope and use addendums.
distractors.trap: Addendums are too rigid for late best-effort scope.

distractors.text: Document a standard change control process in the contract.
