distractors.trap: Over-weights quality when schedule is the binding constraint.

distractors.text: Past performance overall, selecting the most historically reliable bidder in the pool.
distractors.trap: History is an input, not decisive under a hard deadline.

distractors.text: Lowest price among the bidders, in order to maximize short-term cost savings.
distractors.trap: Cheapest bid ignores schedule risk from inconsistent delivery.

distractors.text: Contract flexibility that allows unlimited future scope changes without renegotiation.
distractors.trap: Flexibility is not the primary constraint in this scenario.

distractors.text: Geographic proximity of the supplier's headquarters relative to the project site.
distractors.trap: Location does not outweigh delivery risk to a hard launch.

distractors.text: The supplier's willingness to accept the lowest possible payment terms offered.
distractors.trap: Payment terms do not secure the binding launch date.

explanation: Reflects the Planning and Delivery performance domains: source-selection criteria should be weighted toward the project's dominant constraint—here, schedule.

prompts: A large infrastructure program relies on several international suppliers whose performance varies, threatening cost and schedule. Amid complex cross-border rules, you need continuous oversight and the ability to intervene quickly. Which monitoring approach best fits?

An international infrastructure program depends on multiple suppliers with uneven performance that jeopardizes cost and schedule. Cross-border rules are complex, and you need continuous visibility plus fast intervention. What monitoring approach is best?

With several global suppliers on a major infrastructure effort showing variable results, cost and schedule are at risk. You need ongoing oversight under complex multi-jurisdiction rules and quick ability to act. Which approach fits best?

A multi-country infrastructure program faces inconsistent supplier performance threatening budget and dates. You require continuous monitoring and rapid intervention despite complex cross-border constraints. What is the best monitoring method?

correct.text: A centralized supplier dashboard with real-time updates and automated contract-term alerts.
correct.why: Centralized real-time data provides one truth and enables timely multi-supplier intervention.

correct.text: One centralized live dashboard of supplier status with automated alerts on contract breaches.
correct.why: Real-time central visibility supports quick action across many international suppliers.

correct.text: A single real-time supplier dashboard with automated alerts for contract-term deviations.
correct.why: Continuous centralized monitoring enables prompt intervention before issues compound.

distractors.text: Quarterly supplier audits reviewing financial stability, quality controls, and compliance posture.
distractors.trap: Quarterly cadence is too infrequent for timely intervention.

distractors.text: A per-supplier risk matrix updated weekly on delivery, quality, compliance, and cost variance.
distractors.trap: Weekly updates still lag real-time issues in a fast program.

distractors.text: A dedicated team managing each supplier independently and reporting results only monthly.
distractors.trap: Independent silos and monthly reporting fragment oversight.

distractors.text: Annual vendor scorecards shared only at executive governance meetings after year-end close.
distractors.trap: Annual cycles cannot support continuous oversight or rapid action.

distractors.text: Informal email check-ins with each supplier whenever performance issues surface organically.
distractors.trap: Reactive, fragmented contact lacks continuous centralized visibility.

distractors.text: End-of-phase supplier reviews conducted only after each major milestone has already completed.
distractors.trap: Milestone-only reviews delay intervention between gate reviews.

explanation: Supports the Measurement performance domain—procurement control uses timely, centralized metrics to trigger corrective action.

prompts: During contract negotiation, what is the purpose of an escalation clause?
In procurement contract talks, what does an escalation clause primarily accomplish?
What is the main purpose of including an escalation clause in a negotiated contract?
When structuring a seller agreement, why would parties include an escalation clause?

correct.text: To adjust contract prices according to predefined indexes or criteria.
correct.why: Escalation clauses let prices move with agreed indexes for inflation or cost swings.

correct.text: To modify prices based on agreed indexes or predefined adjustment criteria.
correct.why: They allow controlled price changes tied to objective indexes or rules.

correct.text: To permit price adjustments linked to specified indexes or established criteria.
correct.why: Escalation clauses handle indexed cost changes without rewriting the whole deal.

distractors.text: To give either party immediate termination rights under the agreement.
distractors.trap: Describes a termination clause, not price escalation.

distractors.text: To limit the work strictly to the original agreed scope of services.
distractors.trap: Describes a scope limitation, not price adjustment.

distractors.text: To set financial penalties when agreed project deadlines or key milestones are missed.
distractors.trap: Describes liquidated damages, not price escalation.

distractors.text: To transfer all residual project risk permanently from seller to buyer.
distractors.trap: Escalation shares indexed cost movement, not total risk transfer.

distractors.text: To require the seller to absorb all material price increases without relief.
distractors.trap: Opposite intent; escalation allows predefined price movement.

distractors.text: To freeze all scope changes for the full remaining duration of the contract.
distractors.trap: Scope freezes are change-control rules, not escalation.

explanation: Reflects Procurement/Planning knowledge—economic-price-adjustment (escalation) clauses manage cost risk on long-duration contracts.

prompts: A water authority will upgrade filtration across 12 plants using detailed specs and known material quantities, but regional labor rates may swing during the 18-month job. It wants a predictable total price that adjusts only for documented labor-rate changes while keeping material and equipment costs fixed. Which contract type fits best?

Upgrading filtration at 12 plants with clear specs and known materials over 18 months, labor rates may shift regionally. The buyer wants a predictable price adjustable only for documented labor changes, with materials and equipment locked. Which contract type is best?

A utility has detailed specs and known material quantities for an 18-month upgrade of 12 filtration plants, but labor rates may fluctuate. It needs a fixed base price that adjusts only via a verified labor index, not materials or equipment. What contract fits?

For an 18-month filtration upgrade across 12 plants with defined scope and material quantities, labor rates may change. The authority wants price predictability with adjustments limited to documented labor-rate movement. Which contract type best fits?

correct.text: Fixed Price with Economic Price Adjustment tied to a verified labor index.
correct.why: FP-EPA locks the base price yet allows adjustments limited to a documented labor index.

correct.text: FP-EPA contract with adjustments restricted to an objective, verified labor index.
correct.why: FP-EPA keeps materials fixed while permitting only indexed labor-rate adjustments.

correct.text: Fixed-price with economic price adjustment limited to a verified labor-rate index.
correct.why: This form preserves overall price predictability with labor-only index relief.

distractors.text: Time and Materials reimbursing actual labor hours at market rates plus all materials used.
distractors.trap: T&M gives no total-cost predictability.

distractors.text: FP-EPA allowing broad adjustments for any cost category.
distractors.trap: Broad adjustments undermine locking material and equipment costs.

distractors.text: Cost Plus Incentive Fee sharing labor savings between the parties.
distractors.trap: Cost-reimbursable shifts cost risk to the buyer despite well-defined scope.

distractors.text: Firm Fixed Price with no provision for any labor-rate movement.
distractors.trap: Ignores the stated need for labor-index adjustment.

distractors.text: Cost Plus Fixed Fee reimbursing all actual costs plus a set fee.
distractors.trap: Open cost reimbursement loses the desired price predictability.

distractors.text: Unit Price with rates renegotiated monthly for every cost element.
distractors.trap: Monthly full renegotiation is not a controlled labor-only index.

explanation: Reflects Procurement/Planning tailoring—contract type should match risk: FP-EPA suits fixed scope with a single volatile, index-able cost.

prompts: Which contract type has the buyer pay the seller a set price for each unit of product or service delivered?
Under which contract type does the buyer pay a fixed rate for each unit of product or service provided?
What contract form sets a predetermined price per unit of deliverable or service completed?
Which agreement type charges the buyer a fixed amount for every unit of work or product delivered?

correct.text: Unit Price.
correct.why: A unit-price contract fixes a rate per unit when total quantity is uncertain.

correct.text: Unit-price contract.
correct.why: Payment is based on a set price for each unit delivered.

correct.text: Unit pricing agreement.
correct.why: The seller is paid a predetermined amount for each unit provided.

distractors.text: Cost-Plus.
distractors.trap: Reimburses actual costs plus a fee, not a fixed per-unit rate.

distractors.text: Time and Materials.
distractors.trap: Pays for hours and materials, not a fixed price per unit.

distractors.text: Fixed-Price.
distractors.trap: Sets one lump sum for the whole scope, not per unit.

distractors.text: Cost Plus Incentive Fee.
distractors.trap: Shares cost outcomes; does not set a per-unit fixed rate.

distractors.text: Firm Fixed Price.
distractors.trap: One total price for defined scope, not unit-based payment.

distractors.text: Cost Plus Fixed Fee.
distractors.trap: Reimburses costs plus fee without a set unit rate.

explanation: Procurement knowledge—unit-price contracts fit scope divisible into measurable units with uncertain total quantity.

topic: Procurement monitoring: joint spec review for rework dispute
prompts: Under a fixed-price contract with an offshore team, monthly deliverables have defined acceptance criteria. After four months most outputs pass, but a portion require lengthy rework; the vendor blames ambiguous requirements while your team blames poor spec compliance. The project is on schedule but rework is eating contingency. What is the best procurement-monitoring action?

A fixed-price offshore engagement has monthly deliverables with acceptance criteria. After four months most items pass, but some need heavy rework. The vendor cites ambiguous requirements; your team cites noncompliance. Schedule is intact, yet contingency is shrinking. What is the best monitoring action?

On a fixed-price contract with an offshore supplier, four months of monthly deliveries mostly pass acceptance, but rework on some items is consuming contingency. Each side blames the other—ambiguity versus noncompliance. What should you do next for procurement monitoring?

Monthly offshore deliverables under a fixed-price contract mostly meet criteria after four months, yet rework on failures is draining contingency. Vendor claims unclear requirements; your team claims poor compliance. Schedule remains on track. Best next monitoring step?

correct.text: Hold a joint specification review to clarify acceptance criteria and set quality benchmarks.
correct.why: A collaborative review fixes the requirements-versus-compliance gap and reduces future rework.

correct.text: Run a joint spec review clarifying acceptance criteria and agreeing measurable quality measures.
correct.why: Shared clarification addresses both ambiguity and compliance views without over-escalating.

correct.text: Facilitate a joint session so both sides align on acceptance criteria and quality measures.
correct.why: Joint alignment resolves the dispute source and protects contingency going forward.

distractors.text: Launch a procurement audit and escalate to the vendor executives demanding immediate staffing changes.
distractors.trap: Disproportionate escalation while the project is still on schedule.

distractors.text: Amend the contract to cut the fixed price and unilaterally adjust payments for rework costs.
distractors.trap: Unilateral, punitive change bypassing change control.

distractors.text: Run a root-cause analysis solely on the vendor's QA process and mandate extra testing layers.
distractors.trap: Assumes one-sided fault, ignoring requirements ambiguity.

distractors.text: Stop all future payments until the vendor accepts full blame for the rework in writing.
distractors.trap: Punitive halt without clarifying the real requirements gap.

distractors.text: Ignore rework costs entirely because the overall project schedule remains on track for now.
distractors.trap: Contingency burn is a real risk even if dates hold.

distractors.text: Replace the offshore team immediately with a new fixed-price vendor without further discussion.
distractors.trap: Premature switch without first clarifying acceptance criteria.

explanation: Reflects the Stakeholder and Delivery domains—collaborative, proportionate engagement preserves the vendor relationship while restoring quality.

topic: Insist on measurable SLA metrics in negotiation
prompts: Negotiating a long-term service contract, the vendor offers performance clauses that are vague and lack measurable outcomes. What is the best response?
In talks for a multi-year service agreement, the supplier's performance language is vague and unmeasurable. What should you do?
A vendor proposes long-term service terms with fuzzy performance clauses and no measurable outcomes. What is the best next step?
During negotiation of an extended services contract, offered performance provisions lack specific, measurable results. How should you respond?

correct.text: Negotiate specific, measurable performance metrics into the contract before signing.
correct.why: Measurable clauses make the contract enforceable and hold the vendor to outcomes.

correct.text: Insist on clear, measurable performance metrics in the agreement prior to signature.
correct.why: Without measurable SLAs you cannot objectively enforce vendor performance.

correct.text: Require measurable performance metrics be added before the contract is executed.
correct.why: Defined metrics enable accountability; vague clauses do not.

distractors.text: Accept the vague clauses and manage performance only through regular status reviews.
distractors.trap: Reviews cannot enforce undefined, unmeasurable terms.

distractors.text: Accept the vague performance clauses in order to build trust with the vendor.
distractors.trap: Trust does not substitute for enforceable, measurable terms.

distractors.text: Sign the agreement now and add detailed metrics after the contract is executed.
distractors.trap: Post-signing additions may not be binding and can be resisted.

distractors.text: Rely on industry norms instead of writing measurable metrics into the contract text.
distractors.trap: Unwritten norms are hard to enforce contractually.

distractors.text: Leave metrics undefined and resolve issues only through informal escalation channels.
distractors.trap: Informal paths fail when terms lack measurable standards.

distractors.text: Postpone all performance metric discussion until the first service year has already ended.
distractors.trap: Defers accountability past the point of leverage at signing.

explanation: Supports Procurement/Planning—clear, measurable acceptance criteria and SLAs must be established during negotiation.

prompts: As you prepare for contract-negotiation meetings with a key vendor, which preparatory action best strengthens your negotiating position?
Before critical contract negotiations with an important supplier, which preparation most strengthens your position?
Which preparatory step best improves your leverage ahead of vendor contract negotiation sessions?
Preparing for talks with a key seller, what action most strengthens the project manager's negotiating position?

correct.text: Develop your BATNA—the best alternative to a negotiated agreement.
correct.why: Knowing your best fallback sets the walk-away point and anchors decisions.

correct.text: Define your BATNA, the best alternative if no agreement is reached.
correct.why: A clear BATNA establishes leverage and a realistic walk-away threshold.

correct.text: Establish your best alternative to a negotiated agreement (BATNA) before talks.
correct.why: BATNA clarity strengthens position and guides when to accept or leave.

distractors.text: List every project requirement in descending priority order before the meeting.
distractors.trap: Useful input, but not the core leverage a BATNA provides.

distractors.text: Prepare an exhaustive list of concessions you can afford to give the vendor.
distractors.trap: Pre-listing concessions weakens rather than strengthens your position.

distractors.text: Compile complete documentation of all prior vendor communications and meeting notes.
distractors.trap: Good records, but not the key negotiation-strategy preparation.

distractors.text: Commit publicly in advance to accepting whatever first offer the vendor makes.
distractors.trap: Signals weakness and removes bargaining flexibility.

distractors.text: Withhold all internal budget figures even from the negotiators on your own team.
distractors.trap: Hides data from your team without creating real leverage.

distractors.text: Start negotiations without any walk-away point so you can remain fully open-minded.
distractors.trap: No walk-away point leaves you vulnerable to a poor deal.

explanation: Reflects negotiation practice in Procurement and Stakeholder engagement—a defined BATNA underpins sound negotiation strategy.

topic: Follow procurement-plan escalation for unresponsive supplier
prompts: During execution, a key supplier suddenly stops responding, putting critical deliverables at risk. Your Procurement Management Plan defines communication protocols and escalation steps. What should you do first?
A critical supplier goes silent mid-execution, threatening key deliverables. The Procurement Management Plan already defines communication and escalation paths. What is your first action?
When a key seller suddenly stops communicating and critical work is at risk, and the procurement plan includes escalation procedures, what should you do first?
Execution is underway when a major supplier becomes unresponsive, endangering critical deliverables. Escalation steps are documented in the Procurement Management Plan. What do you do first?

correct.text: Follow the plan's escalation procedures to reach higher-level management at the supplier.
correct.why: Acting per the agreed plan addresses the breakdown systematically within contractual terms.

correct.text: Execute the documented escalation path to engage higher management at the supplier.
correct.why: Using planned escalation handles nonresponse systematically and contractually.

correct.text: Apply the Procurement Management Plan escalation steps to contact supplier leadership.
correct.why: The plan's escalation process is the first systematic response to silence.

distractors.text: Convene the team immediately to revise the schedule and mitigate the anticipated delay.
distractors.trap: Treats a symptom without addressing the supplier breakdown.

distractors.text: Keep waiting and only monitor the situation while the supplier remains silent.
distractors.trap: Passive; lets risk to deliverables grow.

distractors.text: Replace the unresponsive supplier with an alternative vendor at once without escalation.
distractors.trap: Premature; may breach the contract and add new risk.

distractors.text: File a lawsuit against the supplier before attempting any of the planned escalation steps.
distractors.trap: Legal action skips planned, proportionate escalation steps.

distractors.text: Cancel the contract immediately without using any of the plan's defined procedures.
distractors.trap: Termination without planned escalation is premature and risky.

distractors.text: Reassign the work internally without notifying procurement stakeholders or following the plan.
distractors.trap: Bypasses procurement process and contractual escalation.

explanation: Supports the Delivery and Planning domains—follow the Procurement Management Plan's defined escalation before ad hoc action.

prompts: Reviewing bids for an infrastructure project, you find one bid far lower than the rest but notice it omits some essential deliverable components. What should you do?
On an infrastructure bid review, one offer is much lower than others yet appears to leave out essential deliverables. What is the appropriate action?
While evaluating infrastructure proposals, a significantly lower bid seems to exclude required deliverable components. How should you respond?
A bid for infrastructure work is far below competitors but may omit essential scope elements. What should the project manager do?

correct.text: Contact the bidder to clarify whether those components are included.
correct.why: Seeking clarification gathers facts before judging the bid for a fair decision.

correct.text: Ask the bidder to clarify if the missing essential components are included.
correct.why: Clarify first so evaluation is based on facts, not assumptions.

correct.text: Reach out to the low bidder to confirm whether the omitted components are covered.
correct.why: Verification before acceptance or rejection keeps the process fair and informed.

distractors.text: Add the missing essential components into the bid yourself without asking the bidder.
distractors.trap: Altering a bid without confirmation is improper and risks disputes.

distractors.text: Disqualify the bidder automatically on the grounds that the submission is incomplete.
distractors.trap: Assumes incompleteness without verifying the facts.

distractors.text: Reject all other bids and award the lowest offer immediately without further checks.
distractors.trap: Chases cost while risking scope and quality.

distractors.text: Raise the low bid price yourself so it matches the average of the other offers.
distractors.trap: Unilateral price changes corrupt the competitive process.

distractors.text: Award based on price alone and resolve any scope gaps only after contract signature.
distractors.trap: Post-award scope discovery invites disputes and overruns.

distractors.text: Share the low-bid details with competitors so they can match or undercut the price.
distractors.trap: Leaking bid content violates fair competition and confidentiality.

explanation: Reflects Procurement fairness and the Stakeholder domain—clarify ambiguities objectively before source selection.

prompts: Drafting bid documents for a complex healthcare IT system, your procurement specialist wants highly detailed specs while legal warns that over-prescription limits competition and innovation. How should you frame the specifications?
For complex healthcare IT bid documents, procurement prefers highly detailed specs; legal cautions that over-prescribing reduces competition and innovation. How should specifications be framed?
Preparing a RFP for a complex health IT platform, detailed prescriptive specs are proposed but counsel warns they may stifle competition and innovation. What is the best way to specify needs?
On a complex healthcare IT procurement, detailed technical prescription is favored by some while others warn it limits competition. How should you structure the specifications?

correct.text: State functional requirements and performance criteria, letting vendors propose how to meet them.
correct.why: Performance-based specs preserve competition and innovation while defining outcomes.

correct.text: Define functional needs and performance criteria so vendors can propose solutions that meet them.
correct.why: Outcome-focused specs invite competition without locking a single design.

correct.text: Use functional and performance-based requirements and allow vendors to propose the approach.
correct.why: Functional specs keep innovation open while still setting required results.

distractors.text: Specify exact product models and configurations so that every bid response is identical.
distractors.trap: Over-prescription limits competition and excludes better solutions.

distractors.text: Give only minimal detail and rely entirely on vendor expertise to decide the approach.
distractors.trap: Too little definition risks solutions that miss objectives.

distractors.text: Combine functional needs with mandatory architecture and require justification for any deviations.
distractors.trap: Baseline architecture still constrains innovation more than needed.

distractors.text: List only preferred brand names and forbid any alternate products from competing vendors.
distractors.trap: Brand lock-in restricts competition and innovation.

distractors.text: Omit performance criteria entirely so vendors have complete creative freedom of approach.
distractors.trap: Without performance criteria, outcomes cannot be evaluated fairly.

distractors.text: Copy a single incumbent's detailed design specifications directly into the bid package.
distractors.trap: Incumbent design specs effectively sole-source the work.

explanation: Reflects tailoring and the Planning domain—performance-based requirements balance clear needs with open, fair competition.

topic: T&M with ceiling price for uncertain quantities
prompts: A pharma company hires a CRO for Phase III trials. Protocols are well defined, but patient enrollments and site visits cannot be predicted. It wants to pay fair rates for actual work while capping total financial exposure. Which contract type fits best?

For Phase III trials with a CRO, protocols are clear but enrollments and site visits are unpredictable. The sponsor wants fair rates for actual effort and a hard cap on total spend. Which contract type is best?

A pharmaceutical firm needs a CRO for Phase III work with well-defined protocols but uncertain patient volumes and visits. It seeks actual-work rates plus a limit on total financial exposure. What contract fits?

Phase III trial support has defined protocols yet uncertain enrollment and visit counts. The buyer wants to reimburse actual effort at fair rates while capping overall cost. Which contract type best fits?

correct.text: Time and Materials with a ceiling (not-to-exceed) price.
correct.why: T&M suits uncertain quantities of defined work; a ceiling caps total exposure.

correct.text: T&M contract with a not-to-exceed ceiling price.
correct.why: Uncertain volumes fit T&M; the ceiling protects the buyer's maximum spend.

correct.text: Time and Materials agreement including a not-to-exceed price ceiling.
correct.why: Pays actual effort flexibly while the NTE limit caps financial risk.

distractors.text: T&M with incentives for hitting early enrollment targets.
distractors.trap: Rewards speed instead of capping financial exposure.

distractors.text: Cost Plus Incentive Fee with a ceiling price.
distractors.trap: Adds cost-verification burden and shifts more risk to the buyer.

distractors.text: Cost Plus Fixed Fee with a ceiling price.
distractors.trap: Reimburses actual costs when rates are already known—unneeded overhead.

distractors.text: Firm Fixed Price covering the entire trial regardless of actual patient enrollment volume.
distractors.trap: Fixed total is hard when quantities are highly uncertain.

distractors.text: Unit Price with unlimited quantity and no spending cap.
distractors.trap: Unlimited units without a ceiling leave exposure uncapped.

distractors.text: FP-EPA adjusting for any cost category throughout the trial.
distractors.trap: Broad price adjustment does not fit uncertain work quantities.

explanation: Reflects Procurement tailoring—T&M-with-ceiling matches uncertain quantity plus a hard cost cap.

prompts: In negotiation, a vendor insists on a fixed-price contract for work with high scope uncertainty. What is the best approach for the project manager?
A seller pushes for fixed price even though scope uncertainty is high. How should the project manager respond?
During contract talks, the vendor wants a firm fixed price on work whose scope is highly uncertain. What is the best PM approach?
When a vendor demands a fixed-price agreement despite significant scope uncertainty, what should the project manager do?

correct.text: Discuss the uncertainty collaboratively and consider a Time and Materials contract.
correct.why: T&M flexes as scope clarifies, avoiding mispricing risk on unknown scope.

correct.text: Work through the uncertainty together and evaluate a Time and Materials arrangement.
correct.why: Collaborative discussion plus T&M fits poorly defined scope better than fixed price.

correct.text: Address scope uncertainty jointly and explore Time and Materials as the contract form.
correct.why: High uncertainty makes fixed price risky; T&M adapts as scope becomes clear.

distractors.text: Choose a cost-reimbursable contract without any further effort to clarify the scope.
distractors.trap: Cost-reimbursable without scope control weakens cost incentives.

distractors.text: Insist on a performance-based contract immediately with no further discussion of uncertainty.
distractors.trap: Skips dialogue and ignores the scope-uncertainty root cause.

distractors.text: Accept the fixed-price demand as written in order to stay within the current budget.
distractors.trap: Fixing price on unclear scope invites overruns and disputes.

distractors.text: Award immediately under fixed price solely to lock the vendor into the engagement.
distractors.trap: Locking price early on uncertain scope increases claim risk.

distractors.text: Refuse any contract until the vendor alone absorbs all scope uncertainty without relief.
distractors.trap: One-sided risk dumping is rarely workable or fair.

distractors.text: Convert to a pure handshake agreement with no written terms or defined pricing model.
distractors.trap: No contract form solves uncertainty and weakens control.

explanation: Reflects Procurement tailoring and Stakeholder collaboration—contract type must match the level of scope certainty.

topic: Root cause analysis before selecting a defect solution
prompts: Developing a new medical device, QC finds it occasionally malfunctions under certain conditions, a potential safety risk. The team wants a software patch; some stakeholders want a full redesign; time and budget are tight. What is the best course of action?

QC reports intermittent malfunctions on a new medical device under some conditions—a potential safety issue. Engineers favor a patch; others want full redesign. Time and budget are constrained. What should you do?

A medical device under development sometimes fails in certain conditions, raising safety concern. Options proposed include a quick software patch or a complete redesign, with limited time and money. What is the best next step?

During medical device development, occasional condition-specific malfunctions appear and may affect safety. The team is split between patching software and redesigning fully under tight constraints. Best course of action?

correct.text: Conduct a root-cause analysis to pinpoint the source, then design a targeted solution.
correct.why: True cause analysis avoids weak patches or excess redesign while protecting safety.

correct.text: Perform root-cause analysis to identify the fault, then apply a focused corrective solution.
correct.why: Knowing the real cause guides an effective fix without wasteful extremes.

correct.text: Analyze the root cause first, then create a solution targeted at that source.
correct.why: Root-cause first prevents ineffective quick fixes and unnecessary full redesigns.

distractors.text: Proceed with a full redesign immediately to cover every possible related issue.
distractors.trap: Likely excessive without knowing the actual cause.

distractors.text: Keep the current design and add manufacturing-stage inspections to catch defects after production.
distractors.trap: Screens symptoms without fixing the underlying fault.

distractors.text: Apply the software patch now because it is the fastest and cheapest available fix.
distractors.trap: May not address the real cause and could add new defects.

distractors.text: Ship the device with a user warning and collect more field data after release.
distractors.trap: Does not fix a potential safety defect before release.

distractors.text: Vote among stakeholders and implement whichever option is most popular right now.
distractors.trap: Popularity is not a substitute for causal analysis.

distractors.text: Defer any corrective action until after market launch in order to protect the schedule.
distractors.trap: Delays safety investigation and risks patient harm.

explanation: Reflects the Quality performance domain—diagnose root cause before selecting a remedy, especially where safety is at stake.

prompts: Three weeks from go-live on a healthcare IT rollout, testing finds a data-migration defect affecting 20% of historical records; delaying triggers heavy penalties. The tech lead offers three options, and clinical, IT, and administration stakeholders each weigh patient safety, continuity, and cost differently. How should you structure the decision?

correct.text: Facilitate a multi-criteria decision analysis with stakeholders to weight factors and score each option.
correct.why: A weighted, structured analysis compares options objectively and builds cross-stakeholder buy-in.

distractors.text: Document the options' pros and cons and escalate to the executive panel for direction.
distractors.trap: Abdicates the PM's role to facilitate a recommendation.

distractors.text: Run only a cost-benefit analysis and let the sponsor decide.
distractors.trap: Over-weights finance and sidelines patient-safety input.

distractors.text: Hold an equal-vote poll among departments and implement the majority choice.
distractors.trap: Equal votes ignore differing expertise and accountability.

explanation: Reflects Stakeholder engagement and structured decision-making—MCDA weights competing criteria transparently for consensus.

prompts: Your project is behind schedule and you decide to crash it to accelerate. What should your immediate next step be?
The project is late and you choose crashing as the acceleration approach. What should you do immediately next?
Having decided to crash a behind-schedule project, what is your immediate next step?
You will use crashing to recover schedule on a delayed project. What should you do first after that decision?

correct.text: Analyze the cost and schedule trade-offs that crashing would create.
correct.why: Crashing adds cost; weigh added cost against schedule gain before committing.

correct.text: Evaluate cost-versus-schedule trade-offs expected from the crashing approach.
correct.why: Trade-off analysis confirms whether crash cost is justified by time saved.

correct.text: Assess the cost and schedule impacts of crashing before implementing it.
correct.why: You must understand trade-offs before spending more to shorten duration.

distractors.text: Assign more tasks to the team immediately in order to speed delivery overall.
distractors.trap: More tasks can overload the team without adding output.

distractors.text: Tell the team to work faster right away without any further schedule analysis.
distractors.trap: Pressure without analysis risks quality and morale.

distractors.text: Start working overtime yourself in order to set a personal example for the team.
distractors.trap: Doesn't address the delay's cause and risks burnout.

distractors.text: Cut quality checks from the plan to free up schedule immediately for delivery.
distractors.trap: Sacrificing quality is not disciplined crashing analysis.

distractors.text: Add resources to every activity regardless of whether it is on the critical path.
distractors.trap: Non-critical crashing wastes cost without shortening duration.

distractors.text: Announce a new end date without changing resources, scope, or the critical path.
distractors.trap: Date changes alone do not accelerate the work.

explanation: Reflects the schedule aspect of the Planning domain—evaluate cost-schedule trade-offs before applying crashing.

prompts: You need to bring people onto your project team. Which Project Resource Management process are you performing?
Your project needs staff and materials assigned so work can begin. Which resource management process covers obtaining them?
A construction PM must secure crew members and equipment for the next phase. Which process is that?
When you obtain and assign the people and physical resources the work requires, which process are you in?

correct.text: Acquire Resources.
correct.why: Acquire Resources obtains and assigns the team members and materials needed to do the work.

correct.text: Acquire Resources process.
correct.why: This process secures and assigns human and physical resources required for project work.

correct.text: The Acquire Resources process.
correct.why: Obtaining and assigning needed people and materials is Acquire Resources, not estimating or controlling.

distractors.text: Control Resources.
distractors.trap: Monitors and adjusts physical resources, not obtaining the team.

distractors.text: Create Team.
distractors.trap: Not a defined resource-management process.

distractors.text: Estimate Activity Resources.
distractors.trap: Determines resource needs, not obtaining them.

distractors.text: Develop Team.
distractors.trap: Improves competencies of an existing team, not staffing.

distractors.text: Plan Resource Management.
distractors.trap: Defines how resources will be managed, not securing them.

distractors.text: Manage Team.
distractors.trap: Tracks performance and resolves issues after people are on board.

explanation: Reflects the Resource and Team domains—Acquire Resources is the process for obtaining and assigning team members.

topic: Balance technical debt vs feature delivery
prompts: In a retrospective, velocity has dropped over three sprints because earlier technical debt is forcing rework. The team wants a full sprint on debt; the product owner wants to keep adding features and worries about the release date. As facilitator, what is the best approach?

Sprint reviews show rework from accumulated technical debt slowing delivery. Developers push for a pure debt sprint; the PO insists on feature throughput to protect the launch. How should you facilitate?

An agile team’s throughput fell after three iterations of growing debt. Engineering wants a dedicated cleanup sprint; product leadership wants continuous feature delivery. What is the best facilitation approach?
Velocity is down because unpaid technical debt causes rework. The team prefers full-sprint remediation; the product owner prioritizes new features and the release date. What should you do?

correct.text: Facilitate a balanced mix of debt reduction and features, then update and communicate the release plan.
correct.why: Agile favors sustainable balance over extremes, with transparent replanning for stakeholders.

correct.text: Help the team and PO agree on blended debt and feature work, then replan and share the release outlook.
correct.why: Collaboration yields a sustainable mix; stakeholders need an updated release picture.

correct.text: Guide a joint balance of cleanup and delivery, then refresh and communicate the release plan.
correct.why: Neither extreme alone is best; balance plus transparent replanning supports value delivery.

distractors.text: Split the team into separate debt and feature streams and track each stream independently for the rest of the release.
distractors.trap: Splitting creates silos and undercuts collective ownership.

distractors.text: Keep all feature work exactly as planned and add sustained overtime for the whole team to hold the date.
distractors.trap: Overtime ignores the root cause and breeds more debt.

distractors.text: Halt all feature delivery and dedicate the full team to clearing technical debt before any new work.
distractors.trap: Stopping all value delivery over-corrects and ignores business needs.

distractors.text: Defer all technical debt work until after the release date in order to protect feature scope entirely.
distractors.trap: Ignoring debt usually worsens velocity and release risk.

distractors.text: Let the product owner decide alone and record the choice in the backlog without further team discussion.
distractors.trap: Unilateral decisions skip collaboration the situation requires.

distractors.text: Replace the product owner so engineering priorities alone drive the backlog without any negotiation.
distractors.trap: Role changes avoid the needed balance and shared agreement.

explanation: Reflects the Delivery performance domain and value focus: balance debt and value collaboratively, then replan transparently.

prompts: Over several reporting periods, planned value consistently exceeds earned value while CPI holds at 1.0. What should the project manager focus on?

correct.text: Apply corrective actions to address the schedule variance and recover progress.
correct.why: PV greater than EV means a negative schedule variance; CPI=1.0 shows cost is on target, so the issue is schedule.

distractors.text: Keep the current approach since CPI is on target and watch future performance.
distractors.trap: CPI=1.0 hides the behind-schedule condition that PV>EV reveals.

distractors.text: Rebaseline to bring both CPI and SPI to target, even if it alters the baseline.
distractors.trap: Rebaselining to mask variance is not a corrective action.

distractors.text: Revise the cost management plan because CPI signals a cost problem.
distractors.trap: CPI=1.0 means no cost problem; the deviation is schedule.

explanation: Reflects the Measurement performance domain (EVM): interpret SV versus CV correctly and act on the schedule.

prompts: In earned value analysis, subtracting planned value from earned value (EV minus PV) yields which measure of schedule performance?
Using EVM, what schedule metric do you get when you calculate EV − PV?
Which earned value measure equals earned value minus planned value?
If you compute EV minus PV for your project, which schedule performance indicator are you calculating?

correct.text: Schedule Variance.
correct.why: SV equals EV minus PV and shows whether work is ahead of or behind plan.

correct.text: Schedule Variance (SV).
correct.why: SV = EV − PV compares accomplished work with planned work.

correct.text: The Schedule Variance (SV).
correct.why: Schedule variance is defined as earned value less planned value.

distractors.text: Schedule Performance Index.
distractors.trap: SPI = EV/PV is a ratio, not a variance.

distractors.text: Cost Performance Index.
distractors.trap: CPI = EV/AC measures cost efficiency, not schedule.

distractors.text: Cost Variance.
distractors.trap: CV = EV minus AC measures cost, not schedule.

distractors.text: To-Complete Performance Index.
distractors.trap: TCPI is a future efficiency target, not EV minus PV.

distractors.text: Budget at Completion.
distractors.trap: BAC is the total planned budget, not a variance.

distractors.text: Estimate at Completion.
distractors.trap: EAC forecasts final cost, not schedule variance.

explanation: Reflects the Measurement performance domain: the EVM schedule-variance formula.

topic: EAC formula selection for atypical variance
prompts: Reviewing performance at month four, the team confirms early inefficiencies are resolved and expects remaining work to proceed at the original budgeted rate. Which Estimate at Completion (EAC) formula fits this assumption?
Cost issues early in the project are fixed; remaining work should cost at the planned budgeted rate. Which EAC formula applies?
Variances so far are considered atypical and will not continue. You need EAC assuming remaining work at the budgeted rate. Which formula?
After resolving one-time inefficiencies, management assumes future work matches original budgeted cost rates. Which EAC calculation is appropriate?

correct.text: EAC = AC + (BAC minus EV).
correct.why: When past variances are atypical, remaining work is estimated at the budgeted rate.

correct.text: EAC = AC + (BAC − EV).
correct.why: Atypical variance: actual cost plus remaining budgeted work gives EAC.

correct.text: EAC equals AC plus BAC minus EV.
correct.why: Budgeted-rate EAC uses actuals plus work still planned in the budget.

distractors.text: EAC = BAC / CPI.
distractors.trap: Assumes current cost inefficiency continues, not atypical variance.

distractors.text: EAC = AC + (BAC minus EV) / CPI.
distractors.trap: Dividing by CPI projects current performance, not budgeted rate.

distractors.text: EAC = AC + [(BAC minus EV) / (CPI x SPI)].
distractors.trap: Assumes both cost and schedule trends persist.

distractors.text: EAC = BAC × CPI.
distractors.trap: Multiplies budget by CPI rather than using atypical-variance logic.

distractors.text: EAC = EV / CPI.
distractors.trap: Not a standard EAC formula for remaining work.

distractors.text: EAC = AC + ETC / SPI.
distractors.trap: Mixes schedule index into cost forecast incorrectly.

explanation: Reflects the Measurement performance domain (EVM): select the EAC formula by the variance-typicality assumption.

prompts: What distinguishes the Critical Path Method (CPM) from the Program Evaluation and Review Technique (PERT)?
How does CPM differ from PERT in how activity durations are treated?
In schedule network analysis, what is the key difference between CPM and PERT?
Which statement correctly contrasts Critical Path Method with Program Evaluation and Review Technique?

correct.text: CPM assumes fixed activity duration; PERT uses three estimates for probabilistic duration.
correct.why: PERT models uncertainty with three estimates; CPM treats durations as known and fixed.

correct.text: CPM uses single fixed durations; PERT uses optimistic, most-likely, and pessimistic estimates.
correct.why: Three-point PERT durations are probabilistic; CPM assumes deterministic durations.

correct.text: CPM treats durations as deterministic; PERT derives probabilistic duration from three estimates.
correct.why: The core distinction is fixed versus three-estimate probabilistic activity times.

distractors.text: CPM suits complex, uncertain projects, while PERT suits only simple, fully well-defined ones.
distractors.trap: Reversed: PERT’s probabilistic nature suits uncertainty.

distractors.text: CPM uses only a network diagram, while PERT uses only a Gantt chart for schedule analysis.
distractors.trap: Both use network diagrams; charting is not the distinction.

distractors.text: CPM identifies the critical path, while PERT’s sole purpose is identifying all project risks.
distractors.trap: Both can find the critical path; that is not PERT’s sole purpose.

distractors.text: CPM is only for agile projects, while PERT is only for predictive waterfall-style delivery.
distractors.trap: Neither method is limited to one delivery approach.

distractors.text: CPM estimates project cost only, while PERT estimates only remaining schedule float values.
distractors.trap: Both are schedule network techniques, not cost methods.

distractors.text: CPM always requires Monte Carlo simulation, while PERT forbids any use of probability at all.
distractors.trap: Reverses roles; PERT is the probabilistic three-estimate method.

explanation: Reflects the Planning/Schedule domain: CPM versus PERT differ in how they model activity duration.

prompts: Midway through a complex, innovative software project, quality objectives are not being met despite earlier tests looking fine. Consistent with PMBOK 7's adaptive, improvement-focused quality approach, what should the project manager do?
On an innovative product effort, quality targets are slipping even though early checks seemed acceptable. Following an adaptive continuous-improvement quality mindset, what is the best next step?
Quality goals on a complex R&D-style project are not being achieved. Tests earlier looked fine. What adaptive quality action should the PM take?
Despite prior testing appearing adequate, a complex software initiative is missing quality objectives. What should the project manager do under PMBOK’s improvement-focused quality approach?

correct.text: Run a holistic quality review with stakeholder feedback to find root causes and adapt processes.
correct.why: PMBOK quality stresses continuous improvement and stakeholder-informed adaptation.

correct.text: Conduct a broad quality review with multi-stakeholder input to diagnose causes and improve processes.
correct.why: Adaptive quality seeks root causes and process change, not rigid enforcement alone.

correct.text: Facilitate a holistic quality review with stakeholders to identify causes and adjust approaches.
correct.why: Stakeholder-informed review supports continuous improvement when objectives slip.

distractors.text: Increase audit frequency to enforce the predefined standards only on the biggest deviations found.
distractors.trap: More enforcement of fixed standards ignores adaptation.

distractors.text: Replace the entire quality framework at once with a new cutting-edge approach company-wide.
distractors.trap: Wholesale replacement skips diagnosing causes.

distractors.text: Direct the team to follow the original quality plan strictly with almost no allowed deviation.
distractors.trap: Rigid adherence contradicts adaptive improvement.

distractors.text: Pause all testing activity until new quality tools are purchased, installed, and fully configured.
distractors.trap: Tool purchases alone do not address process root causes.

distractors.text: Outsource quality ownership entirely to a vendor and stop all internal quality reviews immediately.
distractors.trap: Abdicating ownership weakens continuous improvement.

distractors.text: Accept lower quality targets so reported metrics match current performance without process change.
distractors.trap: Lowering targets hides problems instead of improving processes.

explanation: Reflects the Quality performance domain: continuous improvement and adaptation informed by stakeholders.

prompts: Evaluating four RFP responses for fiber installation, the lowest bidder is new but well-referenced, while the second-lowest is a former vendor with strong quality but past minor delays. Policy requires justification for not choosing the lowest compliant bid. How should you proceed?

Four compliant bids for network cabling arrived. The lowest-price offer is a new firm with solid references; a known vendor bid slightly higher with strong quality and minor past delays. You must justify any award not going to the lowest bid. What is the best approach?

Source selection for a fiber install has four RFP responses. Lowest price is a new but well-referenced bidder; second-lowest is a prior vendor with good quality and some delay history. Policy needs documented justification off lowest price. How do you proceed?

You are comparing four fiber-installation proposals. Lowest compliant bid is a new supplier with good references; a former vendor is second-lowest with strong quality but minor delays. Policy requires justifying not selecting the lowest bid. What should you do?

correct.text: Evaluate all bids with weighted criteria covering past performance, technical capability, and cost, then document the scoring.
correct.why: Documented weighted criteria justify best-value awards beyond lowest price alone.

correct.text: Score every proposal using weighted criteria for performance, capability, and cost, and record the results.
correct.why: PMBOK source selection relies on transparent multi-factor evaluation and documentation.

correct.text: Apply documented weighted selection criteria across all bids, including cost and capability, then retain the scores.
correct.why: Best-value selection needs objective weighted scoring, not price or familiarity alone.

distractors.text: Issue a supplemental RFP to attract additional bidders and broaden the pool before applying any selection criteria.
distractors.trap: Four compliant bids already support comparison without reopening.

distractors.text: Award to the second-lowest bidder based on the prior working relationship and track record of acceptable quality performance.
distractors.trap: Familiarity without objective scoring bypasses required evaluation.

distractors.text: Award to the lowest compliant bidder, since policy defaults to lowest price when all bids satisfy the RFP requirements.
distractors.trap: Lowest price alone ignores weighted quality and performance factors.

distractors.text: Negotiate only with the former vendor and discard the other three responses without formal scoring.
distractors.trap: Excluding bids without criteria violates fair source selection.

distractors.text: Pick the highest-priced bid to signal premium quality without applying selection weights.
distractors.trap: Price alone, high or low, is not a full evaluation method.

distractors.text: Defer award and re-solicit next year so more vendors can respond after market changes.
distractors.trap: Unnecessary delay when four valid bids already exist.

explanation: Reflects the Procurement/Planning domain: weighted, documented source-selection criteria.

prompts: A marketing campaign's social-media engagement has fallen over the past month, and the timeline is tight. The project manager wants to use KPIs to turn performance around. Which approach is most effective?
Engagement metrics on a product launch campaign are declining with little time left. How should the PM use KPIs most effectively?
A digital campaign’s social engagement has dropped while the end date is fixed. What KPI approach best supports recovery?
With social engagement sliding and a tight schedule, which way of using KPIs is most likely to improve campaign performance?

correct.text: Identify KPIs aligned with campaign goals and analyze them regularly for data-driven adjustments.
correct.why: Goal-aligned KPIs enable timely, evidence-based corrective action.

correct.text: Select goal-relevant KPIs and review them often to drive evidence-based course corrections.
correct.why: Relevant measures plus regular analysis support measurement-based control.

correct.text: Define KPIs that match campaign objectives and use frequent analysis to adjust actions.
correct.why: Aligned metrics and ongoing review turn data into corrective decisions.

distractors.text: Track a wide range of KPIs to cover everything possible and deliberately reduce deep analysis.
distractors.trap: Too many KPIs dilute focus and delay action.

distractors.text: Focus only on budget KPIs so financial efficiency alone is expected to drive engagement recovery.
distractors.trap: Cost metrics do not measure or improve engagement.

distractors.text: Adopt industry-standard KPIs and assume they will improve results without further adjustment.
distractors.trap: Generic KPIs without analysis do not fix the decline.

distractors.text: Report KPIs only at project close so the team stays fully focused on delivery until the end.
distractors.trap: Late reporting prevents timely corrective action.

distractors.text: Replace all KPIs every month so the dashboard always looks fresh to sponsors and executives.
distractors.trap: Constant metric churn blocks trend analysis.

distractors.text: Stop measuring engagement entirely and rely on team intuition for all creative campaign changes.
distractors.trap: Abandons data-driven control the situation needs.

explanation: Reflects the Measurement performance domain: choose relevant KPIs and adjust based on data.

topic: Root cause + corrective action for quality deviation
prompts: During execution of a high-profile telecom project, you find a significant quality defect in the module handling user data that could breach compliance standards. The charter demands zero compliance tolerance and top quality. Which action best fits PMBOK quality principles while limiting schedule and confidence impacts?

A critical user-data component on a regulated telecom effort has a serious quality defect that may violate compliance. Zero tolerance for compliance gaps is required. What is the best quality-aligned action with limited disruption?

You discover a major defect in a compliance-sensitive data module on a high-visibility telecom project. Quality and compliance must not be compromised. How should you respond under PMBOK quality guidance?

On a telecom program with strict compliance and quality mandates, a significant defect appears in the user-data handling module. What action best addresses quality while containing schedule and confidence effects?

correct.text: Perform root cause analysis with key stakeholders, then develop and implement a corrective action plan.
correct.why: Quality defects need root-cause analysis and corrective action without needless disruption.

correct.text: Engage key stakeholders in root cause analysis, then create and execute a corrective action plan.
correct.why: Finding and fixing the cause protects integrity better than concealment or full stoppage.

correct.text: Analyze root causes with relevant stakeholders and implement a documented corrective action plan.
correct.why: PMBOK quality resolves defects through cause analysis and targeted correction.

distractors.text: Have QA fix the defect quietly without alerting senior management in order to avoid panic.
distractors.trap: Concealment violates transparency and stewardship.

distractors.text: Accelerate unaffected work areas aggressively to offset any schedule delay caused by the defect.
distractors.trap: Ignores the defect and prioritizes schedule over quality.

distractors.text: Halt all project execution immediately, document it, and notify every stakeholder including legal.
distractors.trap: Full stoppage overreacts before the cause is understood.

distractors.text: Ship the release as planned and file a compliance waiver request only after the go-live date.
distractors.trap: Accepts known compliance risk against zero-tolerance rules.

distractors.text: Replace the entire module vendor immediately without first analyzing the underlying defect cause.
distractors.trap: Vendor swap without RCA may miss the real problem.

distractors.text: Downgrade the compliance requirement in the charter so the current defect becomes acceptable.
distractors.trap: Changing standards to fit defects undermines quality integrity.

explanation: Reflects the Quality performance domain: diagnose root cause and apply corrective action.

prompts: You want to confirm the company is profiting from a project. Which KPI best assesses this?
Which performance indicator best shows whether a project is delivering profit relative to its cost?
Leadership asks which KPI most directly indicates project profitability. What should you choose?
To verify the organization is earning a financial return on a project investment, which KPI is most appropriate?

correct.text: Return on Investment (ROI).
correct.why: ROI compares gain against cost, indicating whether the investment is profitable.

correct.text: Return on Investment.
correct.why: ROI directly relates benefits or returns to the cost of the investment.

correct.text: ROI (Return on Investment).
correct.why: Profitability relative to investment is what ROI is designed to show.

distractors.text: Resource Utilization Rate.
distractors.trap: It measures asset usage, not profitability.

distractors.text: Schedule Performance Index (SPI).
distractors.trap: SPI measures time efficiency, not profit.

distractors.text: Cost Variance (CV).
distractors.trap: CV shows cost performance, not overall profitability.

distractors.text: Team Velocity.
distractors.trap: Velocity tracks delivery throughput, not financial return.

distractors.text: Defect Density.
distractors.trap: Quality metrics do not measure profitability.

distractors.text: Customer Satisfaction Score.
distractors.trap: Perception metrics are not direct profit measures.

explanation: Reflects the Measurement domain and business value: ROI indicates profitability.

prompts: Analyzing quality data, you find that about 80% of product defects trace to roughly 20% of the identified issue types. Which quality management concept does this best illustrate?

correct.text: The Pareto principle, focusing improvement on the vital few causes.
correct.why: The 80/20 pattern is the Pareto principle, directing effort to the few causes producing most defects.

distractors.text: Statistical process control, monitoring variation to keep output consistent.
distractors.trap: SPC tracks process stability, not the distribution of causes.

distractors.text: Six Sigma, reducing defects by analyzing key sources of variation.
distractors.trap: Six Sigma is a broad methodology that merely uses Pareto as a tool.

distractors.text: Theory of constraints, identifying bottlenecks limiting throughput.
distractors.trap: TOC concerns flow constraints, not defect-cause distribution.

explanation: Reflects the Quality performance domain: Pareto analysis prioritizes the vital few causes.

prompts: You are calculating the Estimate to Complete (ETC) for a project with significant cost variances, but you assume the remaining work will be performed at the budgeted rate. Which formula should you use?
Cost variances have been large so far, yet you expect remaining work at the original budgeted rate. Which ETC formula fits?
Assuming past cost variances are atypical and will not continue, how do you compute Estimate to Complete?
Significant cost variance exists, but remaining work should follow the budgeted rate. Which ETC calculation applies?

correct.text: ETC = BAC minus EV, the remaining budgeted work.
correct.why: Atypical past variance means remaining work equals BAC minus EV.

correct.text: ETC = BAC − EV for remaining work at the budgeted rate.
correct.why: Budgeted-rate ETC is the unfinished planned work: BAC less EV.

correct.text: ETC equals BAC minus EV under a budgeted-rate assumption.
correct.why: When future work matches the budget rate, ETC is BAC minus EV.

distractors.text: ETC = BAC / CPI, adjusting for current cost trends.
distractors.trap: Dividing by CPI assumes current inefficiency continues.

distractors.text: ETC = (BAC minus EV) / CPI, factoring remaining work and efficiency.
distractors.trap: Applying CPI projects current performance, not budgeted rate.

distractors.text: ETC = AC plus a remaining estimate from historical spending.
distractors.trap: Mixes concepts and is not a standard ETC formula.

distractors.text: ETC = EV / SPI for remaining schedule-adjusted cost.
distractors.trap: SPI is a schedule index, not budgeted-rate ETC.

distractors.text: ETC = BAC × CPI to scale the total budget by efficiency.
distractors.trap: Not the ETC formula for atypical remaining work.

distractors.text: ETC = AC − EV as the residual cost variance to finish.
distractors.trap: AC minus EV is not Estimate to Complete.

explanation: Reflects the Measurement performance domain (EVM): ETC under the budgeted-rate assumption.

prompts: For a satellite program you must procure a custom antenna array. Specs are detailed, but the cutting-edge development may need design iterations over an estimated 8 to 14 months. Procurement prefers T&M, finance prefers firm-fixed-price. Which contract type best balances both while allocating risk appropriately?

correct.text: A Fixed Price Incentive Fee contract, with a ceiling price plus incentives for efficient performance.
correct.why: FPIF caps the buyer's cost while sharing risk and rewarding efficiency, fitting technical uncertainty with budget control.

distractors.text: A Cost Plus Incentive Fee contract, reimbursing costs plus milestone bonuses.
distractors.trap: CPIF lacks a firm ceiling, leaving the buyer substantial cost risk.

distractors.text: A Cost Plus Fixed Fee contract, reimbursing costs plus a fixed fee.
distractors.trap: CPFF places most cost risk on the buyer, with no budget control.

distractors.text: A Firm Fixed Price contract, locking the price and shifting all risk to the vendor.
distractors.trap: FFP over-shifts risk on uncertain work, inflating price or risking quality.

explanation: Reflects the Procurement/Planning domain: match contract type to risk and technical uncertainty.

prompts: During Define Scope, your analyst presents 47 requirements. Operations insists on all of them; IT notes some overlap with a planned system upgrade; and the fixed budget can't cover everything. What is the best approach to set the project scope?
In Define Scope, 47 requirements are on the table. Operations wants them all; IT flags overlap with an upcoming upgrade; the budget is fixed and insufficient for everything. How should you set scope?
You have 47 candidate requirements. Business wants full inclusion, some items overlap a planned upgrade, and funding cannot cover all. What is the best way to establish project scope?
Define Scope faces 47 requirements, fixed funding, partial overlap with a system upgrade, and pressure to include everything. What approach best sets the baseline?

correct.text: Facilitate prioritization to rank requirements by business value and feasibility, documenting baseline versus deferred items.
correct.why: Prioritizing within a fixed budget defines a clear baseline and prevents creep.

correct.text: Run a prioritization workshop ranking requirements by value and feasibility, recording in-scope and deferred items.
correct.why: Not all gathered requirements enter scope; ranking sets a controlled baseline.

correct.text: Lead stakeholders to prioritize by business value and feasibility, then document baseline versus deferred requirements.
correct.why: Fixed budgets require ranked inclusion, not unconditional commitment to all items.

distractors.text: Document all 47 requirements as in-scope with a conditional note that lower-priority items ship only if budget allows later.
distractors.trap: Conditional scope creates an ambiguous, unstable baseline.

distractors.text: Consolidate overlapping requirements IT identified, keep all 47 in scope, and submit a change request for a budget increase.
distractors.trap: Extra funding contradicts the established fixed-budget constraint.

distractors.text: Phase all 47 requirements across two or three stages so every item stays committed but is spread to fit the budget.
distractors.trap: Phasing all items still fails formal prioritization under fixed funds.

distractors.text: Accept only Operations’ full list of requirements and exclude IT from any further scope discussions going forward.
distractors.trap: Ignores constraints and stakeholder input needed for prioritization.

distractors.text: Cut requirements at random until the fixed budget fits without any stakeholder ranking by value or feasibility.
distractors.trap: Arbitrary cuts lack value-based prioritization.

distractors.text: Defer Define Scope entirely until Operations secures a larger budget large enough for all 47 requirements.
distractors.trap: Waiting for more money avoids the fixed-budget prioritization task.

explanation: Reflects the Scope/Planning domain: prioritize requirements to define the scope baseline.

prompts: In a plant upgrade, concrete curing on the critical path is lagging and threatens a regulator-fixed commissioning date seven weeks out. Parallel electrical work has three weeks of float, downstream work depends on both, and the subcontractor expects curing delays to continue. What is the most effective schedule action?

Concrete curing is late on the critical path of a plant upgrade and endangers a non-movable regulatory commissioning date. Electrical work has float; later work needs both paths. Delays are expected to continue. What schedule action is most effective?

A fixed regulator commissioning date is at risk because curing on the critical path is behind. Parallel electrical activities hold float; successors depend on both. The subcontractor expects ongoing curing slip. What should you do?

Critical-path concrete curing is lagging on a facility upgrade with a fixed regulatory finish date. Parallel electrical work has float; downstream depends on both. Curing delays will likely continue. Best schedule response?

correct.text: Prepare a fast-tracking plan to overlap work independent of curing, and seek sponsor approval.
correct.why: Curing time is fixed; overlapping independent work protects a non-movable milestone.

correct.text: Develop a fast-tracking approach that overlaps non-curing-dependent work and obtain sponsor approval.
correct.why: Schedule compression via overlap of independent work addresses immovable dates.

correct.text: Plan fast-tracking to overlap activities independent of curing, then get sponsor approval to proceed.
correct.why: When duration is chemistry-limited, overlapping independent work compresses the path.

distractors.text: Authorize overtime for the curing crew and all parallel teams, then adjust the baseline afterward.
distractors.trap: Overtime cannot shorten curing; float teams add wasted cost.

distractors.text: Amend the construction contract to extend the finish date by seven weeks past the regulator date.
distractors.trap: The regulatory date is fixed; extension is not acceptable.

distractors.text: Resequence curing steps every week and hope the subcontractor eventually recovers the original pace.
distractors.trap: Hoping for recovery creates no reliable time savings.

distractors.text: Crash only the electrical work that already has float in order to free budget for other activities.
distractors.trap: Crashing float work does not protect the critical path.

distractors.text: Remove curing from the schedule network entirely so the reported critical path appears shorter.
distractors.trap: Hiding work from the network is not schedule compression.

distractors.text: Transfer all float from electrical work into management reserve and wait for curing to improve.
distractors.trap: Reclassifying float does not recover lost curing time.

explanation: Reflects the Schedule performance domain: fast-tracking compresses the schedule to protect a fixed milestone.

prompts: During which procurement process does the project manager evaluate seller proposals and select the supplier best suited to the work?
Which procurement process includes evaluating proposals and choosing the seller for the work?
When you obtain seller responses, evaluate bids, and award a contract, which process are you performing?
Selecting the supplier that best fits the project after reviewing proposals occurs in which procurement process?

correct.text: Conduct Procurements
correct.why: Conduct Procurements obtains responses, evaluates bids, selects a seller, and awards.

correct.text: Conduct Procurements process
correct.why: Seller selection and contract award happen in Conduct Procurements.

correct.text: The Conduct Procurements process
correct.why: Evaluating proposals and choosing the supplier is Conduct Procurements.

distractors.text: Plan Procurement Management
distractors.trap: Defines the approach, not actual seller selection.

distractors.text: Control Procurements
distractors.trap: Manages the relationship after award, not initial selection.

distractors.text: Close Procurements
distractors.trap: Finalizes completed contracts, not vendor selection.

distractors.text: Identify Stakeholders
distractors.trap: Maps people and groups, not procurement seller choice.

distractors.text: Develop Project Charter
distractors.trap: Authorizes the project; it does not select sellers.

distractors.text: Monitor and Control Project Work
distractors.trap: Tracks overall work performance, not bid evaluation.

explanation: Tests the Conduct Procurements process within the Project Work performance domain.

topic: Defect density KPI for code quality
prompts: A software squad keeps releasing code with a high number of defects, driving rework and delays. Which KPI should the project manager track to target this problem directly?
Frequent production defects from a development team cause rework and schedule slip. Which KPI most directly addresses code quality?
A product team’s releases are defect-heavy, creating rework. What KPI should the PM track to focus on this quality issue?
High defect counts in released code are slowing delivery. Which KPI best targets that problem?

correct.text: Defect density
correct.why: Defect density measures defects relative to size, highlighting code quality issues.

correct.text: Defect density metric
correct.why: It relates defects to code size, directly reflecting product quality levels.

correct.text: Defect density rate
correct.why: Normalizing defects by size surfaces where code quality needs improvement.

distractors.text: Code coverage percentage
distractors.trap: Coverage shows testing breadth, not defect levels.

distractors.text: Team velocity
distractors.trap: Velocity tracks delivery pace, not product quality.

distractors.text: Customer satisfaction score
distractors.trap: Reflects perception, not internal defect counts.

distractors.text: Schedule Performance Index
distractors.trap: SPI measures schedule efficiency, not code defects.

distractors.text: Resource utilization rate
distractors.trap: Utilization does not measure software defect quality.

distractors.text: Budget at Completion
distractors.trap: BAC is a cost baseline total, not a quality KPI.

explanation: Quality performance domain — choose metrics that reveal deliverable quality, not just output or pace.

topic: Update quality management plan to current standards
prompts: After launch, customer feedback reveals quality gaps your QA never flagged; you discover the standards you applied reflect outdated consumer expectations while newer, more relevant benchmarks exist. What should you do next?
Post-release feedback shows quality shortfalls missed by QA. Applied standards match outdated expectations; current benchmarks are available. What is the best next step?
Customers report quality issues after go-live that QA standards never caught. You learn the quality criteria are outdated versus known modern benchmarks. What should you do?
Launch feedback exposes quality gaps tied to outdated standards, while more relevant current benchmarks already exist. What should you do next?

correct.text: Revise the quality management plan to adopt current benchmarks, then adjust the project to meet them.
correct.why: Aligning the plan with current standards addresses the satisfaction gap’s root cause.

correct.text: Update the quality management plan with current benchmarks and realign project work accordingly.
correct.why: Refreshing standards in the plan then adapting work closes the quality gap.

correct.text: Adopt the current relevant benchmarks in the quality plan and adjust the project to comply.
correct.why: Outdated criteria caused the miss; updating the plan drives needed changes.

distractors.text: Run another full round of quality control against the existing standards to catch any missed defects.
distractors.trap: Retesting outdated standards ignores the real root cause.

distractors.text: Halt the release immediately and run a full market analysis before taking any other action.
distractors.trap: Overreacts when relevant benchmarks are already known.

distractors.text: Hire a third-party QA firm to re-evaluate the product using both the old and new standards.
distractors.trap: Costly and slow when the plan can be updated internally.

distractors.text: Ignore customer feedback because the product already met the original quality plan criteria.
distractors.trap: Dismissing feedback leaves outdated standards unaddressed.

distractors.text: Replace the entire product with a competitor clone without changing the quality management plan.
distractors.trap: Extreme product swap skips updating quality criteria.

distractors.text: Lower quality targets in status reporting so current defects appear within acceptable tolerance.
distractors.trap: Gaming metrics does not adopt better benchmarks.

explanation: Quality performance domain — quality is fitness for current requirements, so the plan must track evolving standards.

prompts: Three subsidiary project managers submit plan updates whose interdependencies affect the master schedule, consolidated budget, and enterprise risk log. The PMO needs a unified view before the quarterly executive review. As the integration lead, what should you do?

Subsidiary PMs propose interdependent plan changes impacting the master schedule, rolled-up budget, and enterprise risks. Leadership wants one integrated picture before the executive review. What should the integration lead do?
Plan updates from three subprojects interact across schedule, budget, and risk. The PMO requires a single coherent view for the quarterly exec session. As integration lead, how do you respond?
Interdependent subsidiary plan changes hit the master schedule, consolidated cost, and risk log. Executives expect a unified assessment. What is your role as integration lead?

correct.text: Analyze how each update affects the integrated baseline and prepare a consolidated impact assessment for review.
correct.why: Integration assesses cross-project effects together and presents one coherent view.

correct.text: Assess each update’s effect on the integrated baseline and deliver a consolidated impact assessment for review.
correct.why: Stakeholders need one integrated view of interdependent plan changes.

correct.text: Evaluate cross-project baseline impacts of the updates and produce a consolidated assessment for stakeholders.
correct.why: A unified impact view is the integration lead’s responsibility here.

distractors.text: Analyze each update's baseline impact, then escalate every flagged conflict to the program sponsor to resolve alone.
distractors.trap: Escalating all conflicts abdicates integration responsibility.

distractors.text: Review each subsidiary update in isolation and approve only those within governance variance thresholds set.
distractors.trap: Isolated review ignores the interdependencies described.

distractors.text: Have each subsidiary project manager present their own updates directly to the executives separately.
distractors.trap: Fragments the unified view the PMO needs.

distractors.text: Reject all subsidiary updates until after the quarterly review is finished to avoid any complexity.
distractors.trap: Blanket rejection avoids integrated analysis of real changes.

distractors.text: Merge the three updates into the master plan without assessing schedule, cost, or risk effects at all.
distractors.trap: Blind merge skips the required impact assessment.

distractors.text: Ask finance alone to approve the changes based only on the single consolidated budget line item.
distractors.trap: Budget-only review ignores schedule and risk interdependencies.

explanation: Integration / Project Work — the integrator consolidates cross-component impacts into a coherent whole before decisions.
