correct.why: Delaying non-critical work removes over-allocation without cost or finish-date impact.

correct.text: Level the resource by postponing either non-critical activity.
correct.why: Leveling uses available float to clear the conflict without extending the project.

correct.text: Apply resource leveling so one float-bearing activity is delayed.
correct.why: Shifting non-critical work resolves contention while protecting the critical path.

distractors.text: Reassign both tasks to another resource without checking skill fit.
distractors.trap: Risks skill mismatch, training, or unavailability.

distractors.text: Let the over-allocated resource decide which task to delay.
distractors.trap: Not a reliable planning decision process.

distractors.text: Request extra overtime hours from the resource to cover both tasks.
distractors.trap: Causes burnout and overtime cost.

distractors.text: Crash both activities so they finish in less calendar time together.
distractors.trap: Adds cost and pressure when float already exists.

distractors.text: Move one activity onto the critical path to force higher priority.
distractors.trap: Unnecessarily increases schedule risk.

distractors.text: Cancel one of the activities entirely to free the resource.
distractors.trap: Scope cut is excessive when leveling float works.

explanation: Planning performance domain: resource leveling delays non-critical activities to resolve over-allocation.

prompts: On an international project, team members become unavailable due to public holidays in their countries. Without changing the deadline or budget, how do you best apply resource smoothing to maintain progress?
A global delivery team will miss work during national holidays in different countries. Deadline and budget must stay fixed. How should resource smoothing be applied?
Public holidays will take distributed team members offline at different times. You cannot change the end date or funding. What is the best resource-smoothing approach?
Across an international program, holiday absences create uneven availability. Keeping deadline and budget fixed, how do you best smooth resources to sustain progress?

correct.text: Redistribute tasks within their float to balance allocation across the available periods.
correct.why: Smoothing uses float so deadline and critical path stay intact around holiday gaps.

correct.text: Shift work inside existing float to periods when the affected people are available.
correct.why: Float-based redistribution smooths load without changing deadline or budget.

correct.text: Balance assignments within free float so holiday unavailability does not force overtime.
correct.why: Smoothing stays inside float, preserving finish date and cost constraints.

distractors.text: Assign extra tasks to available members during the holidays.
distractors.trap: Overloads available staff rather than smoothing.

distractors.text: Reschedule tasks beyond their float even if the critical path changes.
distractors.trap: That is leveling with schedule impact, not smoothing.

distractors.text: Compress tasks during availability, accepting added risk and quality impact.
distractors.trap: Compression raises risk, contrary to smoothing.

distractors.text: Extend the project deadline to cover all holiday absences fully.
distractors.trap: Changes the deadline, which the constraint forbids.

distractors.text: Add temporary staff for every holiday gap regardless of budget.
distractors.trap: Violates the fixed-budget constraint.

distractors.text: Ignore holiday calendars and keep original resource loading.
distractors.trap: Leaves plans inconsistent with actual availability.

explanation: Planning performance domain: resource smoothing redistributes within float without changing the end date.

prompts: Your data-migration specialist is 62% through Phase 2 validation when Phase 3 urgently needs them for underestimated data mapping, and change management also wants them for UAT with a contractual deadline in three weeks. The sponsor says go-live can't slip but data-quality issues would harm reputation. How do you approach this?

correct.text: Assess the impact of each option, then present the trade-offs to stakeholders for a collaborative allocation decision.
correct.why: With competing strategic constraints, impact-based analysis plus stakeholder input aligns the decision with priorities.

distractors.text: Split the specialist equally across all three demands to make partial progress everywhere.
distractors.trap: Context-switching delays every workstream and may miss deadlines.

distractors.text: Prioritize UAT because the contractual deadline creates legal obligations.
distractors.trap: Assumes contract automatically trumps reputational data-quality risk.

distractors.text: Move the specialist to Phase 3 and continue Phase 2 with remaining staff.
distractors.trap: Reactive; ignores UAT and data-quality implications.

explanation: Stakeholder performance domain: resolve competing priorities through impact analysis and collaborative decision-making.

prompts: On a CRM implementation, the required number of database administrators exceeds availability due to overlapping project schedules. What is the best way to adjust your resource forecast?
A customer platform rollout needs more DBAs than are free because other projects overlap. How should you best adjust the resource forecast?
Your enterprise CRM program faces a DBA shortfall caused by concurrent schedules elsewhere. What is the best forecast adjustment approach?
Overlapping initiatives have left fewer database administrators than your CRM plan assumes. What is the best way to revise the resource forecast?

correct.text: Reallocate DBAs from lower-priority tasks and negotiate timelines with stakeholders.
correct.why: Optimizing existing resources and renegotiating timelines resolves shortage without waste.

correct.text: Shift DBAs off lower-priority work and renegotiate affected dates with stakeholders.
correct.why: Reallocation plus timeline negotiation addresses shortage using current capacity.

correct.text: Prioritize scarce DBA capacity toward critical work and align schedules with stakeholders.
correct.why: Rebalancing demand and negotiated timing is the practical first fix.

distractors.text: Increase the budget to hire temporary DBAs and outsource the excess workload.
distractors.trap: May be infeasible or not cost-effective.

distractors.text: Keep the current allocation and extend the project deadline.
distractors.trap: Accepts avoidable delay without rebalancing demand.

distractors.text: Deploy automated database tools to reduce reliance on DBAs.
distractors.trap: No immediate relief for the current shortage.

distractors.text: Remove database work from the CRM scope to eliminate the need for DBAs.
distractors.trap: Scope cuts avoid the constraint rather than managing it.

distractors.text: Assign application developers to perform all DBA-critical activities.
distractors.trap: Skill mismatch risks quality and compliance issues.

distractors.text: Freeze all other projects until this CRM rollout finishes its DBA work.
distractors.trap: Overly disruptive portfolio action as a first response.

explanation: Planning performance domain: rebalance existing resources and negotiate the schedule before adding cost.

prompts: Your network architect with rare legacy-integration expertise may leave within a month for a competing offer. Their critical work falls in months three and four, schedule flexibility is minimal, and replacements take 8-10 weeks to recruit. What contingency should you prioritize?

correct.text: Accelerate knowledge transfer and documentation while exploring retention incentives and starting parallel recruitment.
correct.why: A multi-pronged response captures knowledge, attempts retention, and prepares a replacement given the long lead time.

distractors.text: Substantially raise the architect's salary and assume retention will succeed.
distractors.trap: Puts all eggs in one basket; departure may be non-monetary.

distractors.text: Compress the schedule to finish the critical work early and reassign the rest to juniors.
distractors.trap: Compression and junior handoff add quality risk.

distractors.text: Have an external firm shadow the architect to document knowledge gaps.
distractors.trap: Narrow; ignores retention and replacement options.

explanation: Uncertainty (Risk) domain: address key-person risk with layered mitigations, not a single bet.

prompts: A marketing project's contract data analyst, booked for two dashboards this month, accepts a three-day industry conference that overlaps the build. How should you reflect this in the resource calendar?
Your contracted analyst is scheduled on two dashboard builds but will attend a three-day industry conference during that window. How should the resource calendar be updated?
A freelance data analyst booked for this month's reporting work plans a three-day conference that conflicts with the build. How should you reflect this on the resource calendar?
During dashboard delivery, a contracted analyst will be away three days at a conference. What is the correct resource-calendar treatment?

correct.text: Block the analyst's conference dates on the calendar and move the linked tasks to later slots.
correct.why: The calendar must show true unavailability so dependent work reschedules accurately.

correct.text: Mark the conference period as unavailable and reschedule the dependent dashboard tasks accordingly.
correct.why: Accurate unavailability plus rescheduling keeps plans realistic.

correct.text: Record the three conference days as unavailable and shift the linked work to open dates.
correct.why: Blocking true unavailability prevents phantom capacity in the schedule.

distractors.text: Mark partial availability and have the team accelerate preceding tasks to compensate.
distractors.trap: The analyst is fully occupied at the conference.

distractors.text: Add the conference to the risk log and leave the calendar unchanged.
distractors.trap: Leaves the schedule assuming the analyst is working.

distractors.text: Shift the work to another analyst but leave the current booking in place.
distractors.trap: Creates double-booking and confusion.

distractors.text: Keep the original dates and expect the analyst to complete work after hours.
distractors.trap: Assumes overtime availability that is not planned.

distractors.text: Delete the dashboard tasks from the schedule until the conference ends.
distractors.trap: Overreacts; tasks should move, not disappear.

distractors.text: Treat the conference as billable project work on the calendar.
distractors.trap: Misstates availability and misuses the resource calendar.

explanation: Planning and Team domains: resource calendars must show true availability to keep the schedule realistic.

prompts: On a fixed-schedule software upgrade, a senior developer is scheduled on several non-critical tasks that could be reallocated. What is the most suitable use of resource smoothing here?
A fixed end-date application upgrade overloads a senior developer with non-critical tasks that have float. How should resource smoothing best be applied?
During a fixed-schedule platform upgrade, a senior developer carries several non-critical assignments that can move. What is the most suitable smoothing action?
Your software upgrade cannot slip, and a senior developer is loaded with non-critical work that has schedule flexibility. What is the best resource-smoothing use?

correct.text: Delay the non-critical tasks to periods of lower resource load to ease the developer's workload.
correct.why: Smoothing shifts non-critical work within float without exceeding resource limits.

correct.text: Move non-critical assignments into lower-demand windows using available float.
correct.why: Float-based shifts ease peak load while protecting the fixed schedule.

correct.text: Respread the non-critical work into quieter periods so the senior is not over-peaked.
correct.why: Smoothing balances demand within float on a fixed end date.

distractors.text: Schedule the non-critical tasks concurrently with high-priority tasks to maximize hours.
distractors.trap: Increases the peak load rather than smoothing it.

distractors.text: Add junior developers to assist, raising the peak resource load.
distractors.trap: Adds load rather than leveling demand.

distractors.text: Advance the non-critical tasks ahead of the high-priority work.
distractors.trap: Ignores priority alignment and can overload.

distractors.text: Extend the project end date so all tasks can run without conflict.
distractors.trap: Violates the fixed-schedule constraint.

distractors.text: Crash critical-path tasks to free the senior developer sooner.
distractors.trap: Compression is not the smoothing technique described.

distractors.text: Remove all non-critical tasks from the upgrade scope entirely.
distractors.trap: Scope cuts are unnecessary when float can absorb shifts.

explanation: Planning performance domain: resource smoothing shifts non-critical tasks within float to keep demand within limits.

topic: Assess impact before responding to resource change
prompts: A shared database administrator on your software implementation is being reassigned to a higher-priority project next month, cutting his availability on your project from roughly 50% to 20% during a scheduled critical data migration. What should you do first?

correct.text: Update the resource calendar to reflect the reduced availability and assess the impact on the data migration activities.
correct.why: You must understand the change's effect on the schedule before choosing any response or negotiation.

distractors.text: Update the resource calendar, then negotiate with the other project managers for shared time.
distractors.trap: Negotiating before quantifying impact; you don't yet know what to ask for.

distractors.text: Update the resource calendar, then escalate to the executive sponsor to resolve the conflict.
distractors.trap: Escalating prematurely, before assessing or attempting your own resolution.

distractors.text: Update the resource calendar, then request additional database resources to hold the timeline.
distractors.trap: Jumping to a solution before the magnitude of impact is known.

explanation: Reflects the Planning and Project Work performance domains: assess a change's schedule impact before selecting a response.

prompts: On a renovation with a fixed move-in date, your electrical crew is over-allocated at 150% in weeks 5-7 while HVAC techs sit near 50%. Several electrical wiring tasks carry 15 days of total float, and the critical path through the server room cannot change. What is the best resource smoothing strategy?

correct.text: Shift electrical wiring tasks with available float to earlier or later weeks to lower the peak while protecting the critical path.
correct.why: Resource smoothing adjusts activities within float without changing the end date or critical path.

distractors.text: Reassign some electrical tasks to HVAC techs, who have basic electrical certification and spare capacity.
distractors.trap: Cross-substitution risks quality and safety; it is reallocation, not smoothing.

distractors.text: Negotiate additional time so the electrical crew can spread work beyond weeks 5-7.
distractors.trap: Extending the timeline is leveling; the fixed move-in date forbids it.

distractors.text: Compress the server-room critical path to create float for redistribution.
distractors.trap: Compression adds cost and the critical path is explicitly fixed.

explanation: Illustrates the Planning performance domain: resource smoothing works within float to keep the schedule baseline intact.

prompts: You notice a recurring pattern of conflicts over shared resources that is causing delays and stretched timelines. What is the best way to address it?
Shared resources keep clashing across teams, producing repeated delays and elongated timelines. What is the best response?
Recurring contention for shared people or equipment is stretching schedules. How should you best address this pattern?
Teams repeatedly fight over the same shared resources, which delays work and extends timelines. What is the best corrective approach?

correct.text: Apply resource leveling to redistribute work and balance shared demand.
correct.why: Leveling reallocates over- and under-used resources to remove recurring contention.

correct.text: Use resource leveling to rebalance assignments and reduce shared conflicts.
correct.why: Redistributing demand across time resolves chronic shared-resource fights.

correct.text: Level shared resources to move work off recurring contention peaks.
correct.why: Balancing load through leveling targets the root of recurring delays.

distractors.text: Eliminate sharing and give every team its own fully dedicated resources.
distractors.trap: Causes under-utilization and waste for scarce resources.

distractors.text: Adopt a first-come-first-served rule for all shared resource allocation.
distractors.trap: Ignores task priority and actual project need.

distractors.text: Assign more work to the teams that are not currently in conflict.
distractors.trap: Overburdens those teams and creates new bottlenecks.

distractors.text: Always award the shared resource to the loudest stakeholder first.
distractors.trap: Politics replaces planned capacity balancing.

distractors.text: Ignore conflicts until they hit the critical path and only then act.
distractors.trap: Allows avoidable delays to accumulate.

distractors.text: Double every shared resource estimate to absorb future conflict risk.
distractors.trap: Inflates plans without fixing allocation timing.

explanation: Reflects the Planning performance domain: resource optimization (leveling) balances demand against availability.

prompts: Your DBA Sarah is needed for critical-path Task L (days 5-12) and Task M (days 8-15), which has 6 days of total float; no additional DBA is available, and she is concerned about context-switching during the overlap. A stakeholder suggests running both in parallel. What is the best response?

correct.text: Reschedule Task M to start after Task L finishes, using its float to remove the overlap while protecting the critical path.
correct.why: Delaying a task within its float resolves the conflict without extending the project.

distractors.text: Reschedule Task L to follow Task M, using float to keep resource use optimal.
distractors.trap: Task L is on the critical path; delaying it slips the whole project.

distractors.text: Present a cost-benefit case to the IT director for parallel execution with task segmentation.
distractors.trap: No DBA is available; one person cannot run the tasks truly in parallel.

distractors.text: Split Sarah's hours across both tasks and extend both durations proportionally.
distractors.trap: Context-switching hurts productivity and extending Task L delays the project.

explanation: Demonstrates the Planning domain's resource leveling: delay a task within its float to resolve a conflict without touching the critical path.

prompts: You find that your resource calendar no longer matches the actual availability of your delivery specialists because of unforeseen personal commitments. What is the best next step?
Unplanned personal commitments mean your delivery specialists' real availability no longer matches the resource calendar. What should you do next?
Your schedule still shows specialists as available, but personal commitments have reduced their true capacity. What is the best next step?
A mismatch appears between the resource calendar and delivery specialists' actual availability after unexpected personal commitments. What should you do first?

correct.text: Update the resource calendar to reflect their actual availability.
correct.why: An accurate calendar is the basis for realistic scheduling and reallocation.

correct.text: Correct the resource calendar so it shows the specialists' true availability.
correct.why: Planning and reallocation depend on a calendar that matches reality.

correct.text: Revise the calendar entries to capture current specialist availability accurately.
correct.why: Stale availability data undermines every schedule decision that follows.

distractors.text: Expand the project scope to absorb the availability changes.
distractors.trap: Scope change does not fix availability and adds strain.

distractors.text: Seek replacements for the unavailable specialists immediately.
distractors.trap: Premature before updating calendars and reassessing needs.

distractors.text: Rebuild the schedule from the original, now-inaccurate calendar.
distractors.trap: Planning on stale data yields unrealistic commitments.

distractors.text: Leave the calendar unchanged and hope availability improves soon.
distractors.trap: Preserves false capacity in all downstream plans.

distractors.text: Reduce quality criteria so less specialist time is required.
distractors.trap: Quality cuts do not correct calendar accuracy.

distractors.text: Mark the specialists fully available and book overtime by default.
distractors.trap: Assumes capacity that personal commitments removed.

explanation: Reflects the Planning performance domain: keep resource baselines accurate before adjusting schedule or resources.

prompts: You've inherited a project that is already late and over budget, and stakeholders want an updated resource estimate they can trust. Which estimating technique best fits?
A troubled project you just took over is late and over budget. Stakeholders need a credible updated resource estimate. Which technique is best?
After inheriting a delayed, over-budget effort, leadership asks for a trustworthy resource re-estimate. Which estimating technique best fits?
Stakeholders demand a defensible resource estimate for a project that is already late and overspending. Which technique should you use?

correct.text: Bottom-up estimating.
correct.why: Rolling up work-package estimates yields a detailed, defensible reset.

correct.text: Bottom-up estimating across work packages.
correct.why: Detailed package-level estimates rebuild trust after variance.

correct.text: A bottom-up resource estimate built from the work packages.
correct.why: Ground-up aggregation produces the credible figure stakeholders need.

distractors.text: Analogous estimating.
distractors.trap: Past-project comparison already failed to match reality here.

distractors.text: Parametric estimating.
distractors.trap: Models and ratios missed this project's true state.

distractors.text: Expert judgment alone.
distractors.trap: Useful input but lacks detail to rebuild a credible estimate.

distractors.text: Rough order of magnitude only.
distractors.trap: Too coarse for a trustworthy recovery estimate.

distractors.text: Three-point estimating without package detail.
distractors.trap: Scenario ranges still need a solid work breakdown base.

distractors.text: Vendor quote averaging with no internal breakdown.
distractors.trap: External quotes alone do not rebuild internal resource plans.

explanation: Reflects the Planning performance domain: bottom-up estimating provides granularity when accuracy and defensibility matter most.

prompts: Your pharmaceutical development project needs expensive laboratory equipment that is in high demand across departments and is essential to critical testing throughout the timeline. How should you approach this constraint to minimize risk while holding the schedule?
Critical testing on a drug-development program depends on costly lab equipment shared across departments and booked heavily. How should you manage this constraint while protecting the schedule?
Expensive shared laboratory equipment is essential for your critical tests and is contested by other departments. How do you minimize risk without slipping the schedule?
A scarce, high-cost lab asset is required for critical tests across your pharma timeline and is heavily demanded elsewhere. What approach best protects schedule while reducing conflict risk?

correct.text: Build a utilization schedule with formal slots reserved by project criticality.
correct.why: Transparent shared scheduling optimizes scarce equipment while protecting critical work.

correct.text: Create a formal reservation system with equipment slots prioritized by criticality.
correct.why: Prioritized shared scheduling reduces conflict and schedule risk for critical tests.

correct.text: Use allocated equipment slots and a criticality-based reservation process.
correct.why: Formal shared access balances demand without abandoning the schedule.

distractors.text: Buy dedicated equipment just for your team to avoid coordinating with other departments.
distractors.trap: Financially inefficient for scarce, expensive assets.

distractors.text: Negotiate exclusive access during critical phases in exchange for internal recognition.
distractors.trap: Monopolizing a shared asset breeds cross-department conflict.

distractors.text: Consolidate equipment work and have senior management mandate exclusive priority access.
distractors.trap: Over-disruptive and relies on force over collaboration.

distractors.text: Skip formal booking and seize the equipment whenever your team happens to be free.
distractors.trap: Creates chaos and blocks other critical work.

distractors.text: Delay all critical testing until other departments fully release the equipment.
distractors.trap: Accepts schedule risk instead of planned shared access.

distractors.text: Substitute unvalidated methods that avoid using the shared equipment entirely.
distractors.trap: May invalidate critical testing requirements.

explanation: Reflects the Stewardship and Systems Thinking principles: optimize a shared organizational resource collaboratively rather than hoarding it.

prompts: You need to analyze the skills and competencies a project requires and match them against the people available. Which resource management tool is most effective?
To compare required competencies with available team members' skills, which resource management tool is most effective?
You must map project skill needs against the capabilities of people you can assign. Which tool best supports that analysis?
Which resource management tool best helps match required competencies to the skills of available people?

correct.text: Skills matrix.
correct.why: It maps required competencies to team skills to expose gaps and guide assignments.

correct.text: A skills matrix mapping needs to people.
correct.why: Competency-to-person mapping is the matrix's core purpose.

correct.text: Skills matrix for competency-to-resource matching.
correct.why: Directly compares required skills with available capabilities.

distractors.text: Resource Breakdown Structure (RBS).
distractors.trap: Categorizes resources by type, not skill-to-need matching.

distractors.text: Responsibility Assignment Matrix (RAM).
distractors.trap: Clarifies roles and responsibilities, not competency analysis.

distractors.text: Resource utilization heat map with skills overlay.
distractors.trap: Not a standard tool; less focused than a skills matrix.

distractors.text: Work Breakdown Structure (WBS).
distractors.trap: Decomposes scope, not people-to-skill matching.

distractors.text: Risk register with resource impacts listed.
distractors.trap: Tracks threats, not competency matching.

distractors.text: Stakeholder engagement assessment matrix.
distractors.trap: Maps influence and interest, not skills.

explanation: Reflects the Team performance domain: a skills matrix aligns competencies with work to plan assignments and development.

prompts: On a citywide smart-lighting upgrade, finance needs union electricians tracked separately from short-term subcontracted technicians for grant reporting, while operations wants spare lifts and vans grouped as rentals. All are currently lumped under 'Installation Teams.' How should you adjust the RBS?

A municipal lighting program currently groups union electricians, subcontracted technicians, and rental lifts/vans under 'Installation Teams.' Finance needs labor types split for grants; operations needs rentals grouped. How should the RBS change?

Grant reporting requires union electricians separate from short-term subcontractors, and operations wants rented lifts and vans under equipment rentals—yet all sit under one 'Installation Teams' RBS node. What adjustment is best?

For a smart-city lighting rollout, 'Installation Teams' mixes union electricians, subcontracted techs, and rented vehicles. Finance needs labor segregation; ops needs a rentals branch. How should you restructure the RBS?

correct.text: Split labor into union and subcontractor branches; add equipment-rentals under assets.
correct.why: Satisfies grant segregation and ops scheduling without mixing labor and equipment.

correct.text: Create separate labor nodes by employment type and an equipment-rentals asset branch.
correct.why: Separates reportable labor types and groups rentals for operations.

correct.text: Segregate union electricians from subcontractors and group lifts and vans as rentals.
correct.why: Meets finance reporting and operational rental tracking cleanly.

distractors.text: Recode subcontractors as equipment and leave electricians under Installation Teams.
distractors.trap: Misclassifies labor as equipment, violating reporting rules.

distractors.text: Merge both labor types under one Field Labor node and track equipment only for finance.
distractors.trap: A single labor node still blocks separate grant reporting.

distractors.text: Reorganize the RBS by project milestone, listing crews and machinery under each phase.
distractors.trap: Mixes categories and loses labor-type segregation.

distractors.text: Keep one Installation Teams node and add grant codes only in the timesheets.
distractors.trap: Leaves the RBS unable to structure the required splits.

distractors.text: Put union electricians under assets and place rentals under labor for symmetry.
distractors.trap: Inverts categories and breaks both finance and ops needs.

distractors.text: Collapse all people and vehicles into a single Field Resources branch for simplicity.
distractors.trap: Removes the segregation both parties require.

explanation: Reflects the Planning domain: the RBS must categorize resources to serve both governance reporting and operational use.

prompts: Facing unforeseen difficulties and possible resource shortfalls, you re-estimate using optimistic, most likely, and pessimistic values to produce a more accurate figure. Which technique are you using?
To account for uncertainty and possible resource shortfalls, you rebuild an estimate from optimistic, most likely, and pessimistic values. Which technique is this?
You incorporate optimistic, most likely, and pessimistic estimates after unexpected difficulties appear. Which estimating technique are you applying?
Given uncertainty and potential resource gaps, your revised estimate blends three scenarios: optimistic, most likely, and pessimistic. Which technique is in use?

correct.text: Three-point estimation.
correct.why: It blends optimistic, most likely, and pessimistic estimates to handle uncertainty.

correct.text: Three-point estimating.
correct.why: Uses three scenario values to produce a more realistic estimate under uncertainty.

correct.text: The three-point estimating technique.
correct.why: Combines best-case, likely, and worst-case figures for accuracy.

distractors.text: Monte Carlo simulation.
distractors.trap: A probabilistic model run, not the three-value estimate described.

distractors.text: Bottom-up estimation.
distractors.trap: Aggregates work packages; does not use three scenarios.

distractors.text: Crashing.
distractors.trap: A schedule-compression technique, not an estimating method.

distractors.text: Analogous estimation.
distractors.trap: Uses past projects, not three defined scenario values.

distractors.text: Parametric estimation.
distractors.trap: Applies statistical models or rates, not three discrete points.

distractors.text: Critical path method alone.
distractors.trap: Schedules network logic; is not the three-value technique.

explanation: Reflects the Planning domain: three-point estimation quantifies estimate uncertainty via a weighted range.

prompts: Three concurrent ERP workstreams all need database administration in weeks 8-12, but your two DBAs are over capacity by roughly 50%. The go-live date is fixed and the budget has little flexibility. Which strategy best fits?

correct.text: Negotiate with functional managers to temporarily borrow another DBA and adjust task sequencing to reduce peak demand.
correct.why: Leverages internal resources and smoothing within the fixed date and limited budget.

distractors.text: Bring in an external database consulting firm and set up knowledge transfer.
distractors.trap: Premium external cost conflicts with limited budget flexibility.

distractors.text: Apply resource leveling and shift milestone dates to spread the workload.
distractors.trap: Moving milestones violates the fixed go-live mandate.

distractors.text: Have the two DBAs work extended hours with extra pay through the peak.
distractors.trap: Overtime for a 50% gap risks burnout and error in critical work.

explanation: Reflects the Planning domain: combine internal resource acquisition and smoothing to resolve a shortfall within fixed constraints.

prompts: For a phased hospital renovation with scope defined, activities range from work needing certified medical-facility contractors to tasks for general crews, and you must justify every resource allocation to the board within an 18-month limit. Which approach best yields defensible estimates?

A phased hospital renovation has defined scope, mixed specialized medical-facility work and general crew tasks, and an 18-month limit. The board wants every resource allocation justified. Which approach is best?

You must produce defensible resource estimates for a phased hospital remodel—specialized certified contractors for some work, general crews for other—under an 18-month constraint for board review. What approach is best?
Scope is set on a multi-phase hospital renovation with varied skill needs, an 18-month limit, and board-level scrutiny of each resource allocation. Which estimating approach best fits?

correct.text: Decompose phases into work packages, estimate each, and roll up the total.
correct.why: Bottom-up captures varied complexity and gives line-item justification for the board.

correct.text: Estimate resources per work package by phase, then aggregate to a project total.
correct.why: Package-level detail supports defensible board justification across mixed work.

correct.text: Build a bottom-up estimate from package-level resources in each phase.
correct.why: Bottom-up aggregation handles specialized and general work transparently.

distractors.text: Apply standard healthcare crew-size ratios against the overall project duration only.
distractors.trap: Top-down ratios ignore specialized, varied work.

distractors.text: Rely on senior hospital administrators' operational view of resource needs alone.
distractors.trap: Operational input is not construction estimating expertise.

distractors.text: Use parametric square-footage metrics from general commercial renovations only.
distractors.trap: Generic metrics miss medical-facility specialization.

distractors.text: Copy resource counts from the last office remodel the firm completed as-is.
distractors.trap: Analogous office work is not a medical-facility match.

distractors.text: Set a single lump-sum labor number and refine details only after board approval.
distractors.trap: Fails the need to justify every allocation now.

distractors.text: Average three vendor ballpark quotes without decomposing the renovation work.
distractors.trap: Ballparks lack package-level defensibility for the board.

explanation: Reflects the Planning domain: bottom-up estimating provides accuracy and defensibility for complex, varied scope.

prompts: Midway through an infrastructure upgrade, you find your senior structural engineer spends about three-quarters of their time on routine documentation that junior staff could handle. What is the most effective resource adjustment?
During an infrastructure upgrade, a senior structural engineer spends most of their time on routine documentation suitable for juniors. What is the most effective adjustment?
You discover a senior structural engineer is largely occupied with junior-level documentation on an infrastructure program. What resource adjustment is most effective?
On a midstream infrastructure project, senior structural talent is consumed by routine documentation juniors could do. What is the best resource fix?

correct.text: Move routine documentation to juniors so the senior focuses on critical design.
correct.why: Matching skill level to task value maximizes expertise and develops junior staff.

correct.text: Reassign routine docs to juniors; keep the senior on high-value structural design.
correct.why: Skill-to-task matching frees scarce expertise for critical work.

correct.text: Shift junior-level documentation off the senior onto critical design work.
correct.why: Aligns expertise with complexity and builds junior capability.

distractors.text: Rotate all engineers through documentation on a fixed schedule purely for fairness.
distractors.trap: Wastes specialized talent on routine work for fairness.

distractors.text: Extend the project timeline to fit the senior engineer's current mixed workload.
distractors.trap: Costly delay that leaves the misallocation unsolved.

distractors.text: Hire a second senior structural engineer just to share the specialized design load.
distractors.trap: Expensive; adds resources instead of fixing inefficient use.

distractors.text: Have the senior keep documentation and drop less-visible structural design reviews.
distractors.trap: Sacrifices critical design for routine work.

distractors.text: Outsource all structural design so documentation can remain with the senior engineer.
distractors.trap: Misplaces where scarce expertise should be applied.

distractors.text: Promote junior staff to senior titles without changing any task assignments.
distractors.trap: Titles do not fix skill-to-task misallocation.

explanation: Reflects the Team performance domain: assign work to the appropriate skill level to optimize resource value.

prompts: In planning, demand for skilled resources exceeds supply in certain periods, threatening delays. You want to keep the original project duration. Which optimization technique fits?
Skilled resource demand spikes above supply in some periods and could cause delay. You must preserve the original project duration. Which technique fits?
During planning, peak demand for scarce skilled people exceeds availability. Original duration must stay unchanged. Which optimization technique is appropriate?
You face over-demand for skilled resources in selected periods but will not change the project end date. Which technique best optimizes the plan?

correct.text: Resource smoothing, adjusting activities within their float.
correct.why: Smoothing balances demand using float without changing end date or critical path.

correct.text: Resource smoothing by shifting work only within available float.
correct.why: Keeps duration fixed while reducing peaks inside float limits.

correct.text: Apply resource smoothing so activities move only within their float.
correct.why: Float-bound adjustments preserve original project duration.

distractors.text: Resource leveling to modify the schedule.
distractors.trap: Leveling can extend duration and shift the critical path.

distractors.text: Crashing to accelerate tasks.
distractors.trap: Adds cost to shorten work; does not balance demand.

distractors.text: Critical Chain Method with buffers.
distractors.trap: Adds buffers and can change the schedule structure.

distractors.text: Fast tracking by overlapping critical activities.
distractors.trap: Raises risk; does not optimize resource peaks with float.

distractors.text: Monte Carlo analysis of cost contingency only.
distractors.trap: Analyzes uncertainty; is not a resource optimization technique.

distractors.text: Scope reduction to eliminate resource-heavy deliverables.
distractors.trap: Changes scope instead of optimizing within duration.

explanation: Reflects the Planning domain: resource smoothing balances demand within float while preserving the schedule baseline.

prompts: Weekly metrics show network engineers forecast at 190% during a single cut-over weekend, but they have slack in the two prior weeks. Float is minimal and the sponsor wants cost held steady. Which action best controls the overload while holding scope, budget, and quality?

correct.text: Advance low-risk network tasks into the earlier slack periods to smooth the workload and spread the peak.
correct.why: Smoothing within existing slack relieves the peak without added cost or baseline changes.

distractors.text: Escalate and delay network work to final cutover, treating overtime as tolerable contingency.
distractors.trap: Defers action and concentrates load further into the peak.

distractors.text: Authorize paid overtime for the peak weekend and report the schedule on track.
distractors.trap: Adds cost the sponsor forbids and ignores the histogram signal.

distractors.text: Hire two short-term contractors and crash adjacent activities.
distractors.trap: Raises cost, breaching the hold-cost-steady constraint.

explanation: Reflects the Planning domain: resource smoothing uses slack to level peaks while protecting cost, scope, and quality baselines.

topic: Closing a skills gap under constraints
prompts: Midway through building a predictive-analytics tool, you find the team lacks predictive-modeling expertise. A tight deadline and limited budget rule out hiring or outsourcing. What is the most effective way to obtain the expertise?
Your predictive-analytics build is underway, but the team lacks modeling expertise. Hiring and outsourcing are off the table due to time and budget. What is the most effective way to gain the skill?
A skills gap in predictive modeling appears mid-project. Deadline and budget forbid hiring or outsourcing. How should you obtain the needed expertise?
While developing a predictive-analytics product, you discover missing predictive-modeling skill. Constraints block hire and outsource options. What is the most effective approach?

correct.text: Upskill the existing team in predictive modeling through internal workshops.
correct.why: Training builds the missing capability within budget and timeline constraints.

correct.text: Close the gap by training the current team via internal predictive-modeling workshops.
correct.why: Internal upskilling fits when hiring and outsourcing are ruled out.

correct.text: Develop the needed modeling skill in-house through structured workshops for the team.
correct.why: Building capability on the existing team respects cost and schedule limits.

distractors.text: Significantly delay the project to recruit someone with the expertise.
distractors.trap: Delay violates the deadline and hiring is ruled out.

distractors.text: Redistribute tasks, assigning modeling to the most suitable current members.
distractors.trap: Reshuffling does not create expertise the team lacks.

distractors.text: Outsource the modeling component to a specialized firm.
distractors.trap: Outsourcing is explicitly ruled out by constraints.

distractors.text: Hire a senior modeler on a short-term contract immediately.
distractors.trap: Hiring is forbidden by the stated constraints.

distractors.text: Drop predictive modeling from the product to avoid the skills gap.
distractors.trap: Removes required capability instead of obtaining it.

distractors.text: Wait for the gap to close as people learn informally on the job only.
distractors.trap: Passive hope is unreliable under a tight deadline.

explanation: Reflects the Team performance domain: develop team capability to close a skills gap within project constraints.

prompts: Your data-center upgrade closely resembles several well-documented projects from the past five years, and stakeholders want a preliminary resource estimate within a week. Which approach gives the most reliable initial estimate?
A data-center upgrade is very similar to several documented projects from the last five years, and leadership needs a preliminary resource estimate in one week. Which approach is most reliable?
Stakeholders need a credible preliminary resource estimate within a week for a data-center upgrade that closely matches recent, well-documented comparable projects. Which technique fits best?
You must produce a reliable initial resource estimate in about a week for a data-center upgrade that strongly resembles several past documented efforts. Which approach is best?

correct.text: Analogous estimation, using documented resource data from the comparable past projects.
correct.why: Historical comparables yield a fast, credible preliminary estimate.

correct.text: Analogous estimating based on resource data from the similar prior projects.
correct.why: Strong historical matches support a quick, trustworthy initial figure.

correct.text: Use analogous estimation drawing on the documented comparable projects.
correct.why: Leverages reliable past data when time allows only a preliminary estimate.

distractors.text: Three-point estimation using optimistic, pessimistic, and most likely values.
distractors.trap: Refines activity uncertainty; does not exploit strong historical data.

distractors.text: Parametric estimation using statistical algorithms on output characteristics.
distractors.trap: Needs models when direct comparables already exist.

distractors.text: Bottom-up estimation aggregating each work package individually.
distractors.trap: Accurate but too slow for a one-week preliminary estimate.

distractors.text: Monte Carlo simulation of every activity before any baseline exists.
distractors.trap: Heavy analysis is unnecessary for a fast preliminary figure.

distractors.text: Expert judgment with no reference to the documented past projects.
distractors.trap: Ignores the strong historical data available.

distractors.text: Vendor reverse auctions to discover labor rates only.
distractors.trap: Pricing tactics are not a full resource estimating approach.

explanation: Reflects the Planning domain: analogous estimating is fast and reliable when good historical data exists and time is short.

topic: Updating the RBS for a new resource type
prompts: During design, your team identifies a resource type that wasn't in the Resource Breakdown Structure, and it significantly changes the resource landscape. What is the best next step for the RBS?

Midway through design on a manufacturing rollout, planners discover a specialized resource type missing from the Resource Breakdown Structure. The find materially reshapes resource needs. What should you do with the RBS?
A healthcare IT project uncovers a resource category omitted from the RBS during design, and it substantially alters the resource picture. What is the best next action for the RBS?
While refining the design, the team surfaces a resource type absent from the Resource Breakdown Structure that significantly changes planning. How should the RBS be handled?

correct.text: Update the RBS to add the new resource type under its appropriate category.
correct.why: A current RBS keeps resource planning, monitoring, and control accurate.

correct.text: Revise the Resource Breakdown Structure to include the new type in the right category.
correct.why: Updating the RBS maintains accurate resource planning and control.

correct.text: Add the overlooked resource type to the existing RBS under the proper category.
correct.why: Keeping the RBS current supports reliable resource management.

distractors.text: Tell stakeholders about the change without editing the RBS, to limit disruption.
distractors.trap: Leaves the planning tool inaccurate and unmanaged.

distractors.text: Proceed with the RBS unchanged to avoid rework.
distractors.trap: Ignores a real need, risking resource shortfalls.

distractors.text: Build an entirely new RBS centered on the overlooked resource.
distractors.trap: Wasteful rework versus updating the existing structure.

distractors.text: Wait until baseline approval before reflecting the new resource type.
distractors.trap: Delays accuracy when planning already needs the update.

distractors.text: Track the new resource type only in a separate informal checklist.
distractors.trap: Sidesteps the official structure used for planning.

distractors.text: Remove related categories from the RBS until the design stabilizes.
distractors.trap: Degrades the RBS instead of integrating the type.

explanation: Reflects the Planning domain: maintain the RBS so resource categories stay accurate and controllable.

prompts: Your QA testing servers have run at about 96% memory for two weeks while dev servers average around 50%; the resource plan flags sustained utilization above roughly 90% for evaluation, and user-validation testing in three weeks will add load. IT operations warns of performance degradation. What is the best control action?

correct.text: Analyze the testing workload distribution and work with IT operations to assess capacity expansion or workload balancing before UAT.
correct.why: It honors the plan's evaluation trigger and explores options with the experts before committing.

distractors.text: Log the metrics in the status report and keep monitoring to see if problems appear.
distractors.trap: Passive; the plan's threshold already requires evaluation.

distractors.text: Reallocate dev capacity to testing via virtualization and update the baseline.
distractors.trap: Jumps to one solution and changes the baseline before analysis.

distractors.text: Escalate to the sponsor for budget to buy new testing servers before UAT.
distractors.trap: Premature spend before analyzing lower-cost options.

explanation: Reflects the Measurement and Project Work domains: investigate a triggered threshold with SMEs before selecting a response.

prompts: In the final quarter of a safety-critical air traffic control software project, an audit finds the manual error-logging system is poorly maintained, producing inconsistent records. Robust tracking is needed without derailing the timeline. What should the project manager do?

Near the end of a medical-device firmware release, auditors report that manual defect logs are incomplete and inconsistent. Strong error tracking is required without blowing the schedule. What should the project manager do?
Late in a railway signaling software effort, quality audit findings show manual error logs are poorly kept and unreliable. You need solid tracking without major delay. What is the best action?

A safety-critical control system is in its final quarter when an audit finds manual error logging poorly maintained and inconsistent. How should the project manager improve tracking without derailing delivery?

correct.text: Implement an automated error-logging tool that integrates with the current development environment.
correct.why: Automation gives consistent, real-time tracking quickly without major delay.

correct.text: Deploy automated error logging integrated into the existing development environment.
correct.why: Integrated automation improves consistency fast while protecting the timeline.

correct.text: Adopt an automated logging tool that plugs into the team's current toolchain.
correct.why: Automation restores reliable records quickly without large schedule impact.

distractors.text: Hire an external consultant to assess the system and recommend improvements next quarter.
distractors.trap: Adds delay and cost while the risk persists.

distractors.text: Devote the next sprint to tightening the manual logging process.
distractors.trap: Keeps an error-prone method that already failed.

distractors.text: Delay deliverables to retrain all staff on manual logging.
distractors.trap: Sacrifices schedule for a fragile manual approach.

distractors.text: Accept the audit finding and log defects only after release.
distractors.trap: Leaves safety-critical gaps until it is too late.

distractors.text: Replace the whole quality process with a multi-month redesign.
distractors.trap: Overcorrects and threatens the remaining timeline.

distractors.text: Assign one person to re-enter all historical logs by hand overnight.
distractors.trap: One-off cleanup does not fix ongoing inconsistency.

explanation: Quality performance domain: build quality in with reliable, systemic controls rather than manual effort.

prompts: On a product development project using four-week timeboxes, week-three testing uncovers an architectural flaw affecting three of five planned features; a proper fix needs about two more weeks. A workaround would let all features pass but cause major future rework. The product owner wants to show progress at the review in nine days. How should you proceed while keeping timeboxing discipline?

Your team works in four-week timeboxes. In week three, tests reveal an architectural defect hitting three of five planned features; a real fix takes roughly two more weeks. A shortcut would ship all five but create heavy later rework. The product owner wants visible progress at the review in nine days. How do you preserve timeboxing?

During a four-week iteration, late testing finds an architecture issue affecting three of five features. A proper fix needs about two weeks; a workaround would pass all five now but force major rework. The product owner wants progress shown in nine days. What keeps timebox discipline?

In week three of a four-week timebox, an architectural flaw impacts three of five committed features. Fixing it properly needs about two weeks; a workaround ships everything with painful future rework. Review is in nine days and the product owner wants progress. How should you proceed?

correct.text: Work with the product owner to reprioritize scope, delivering the two unaffected features and a proper architectural fix for at least one affected feature before the timebox ends.
correct.why: Keeps the timebox fixed and flexes scope to deliver done work.

correct.text: Collaborate with the product owner to re-scope the timebox so the two clean features ship and at least one affected feature gets a proper architectural fix.
correct.why: Fixed timebox with flexible scope protects quality and cadence.

correct.text: Reprioritize with the product owner to finish the two unaffected features plus a sound architectural fix on at least one impacted feature within the timebox.
correct.why: Scope adjusts; duration stays fixed and work stays genuinely done.

distractors.text: Apply the workaround to all five features now and schedule the proper architectural fix as high-priority work in the next timebox.
distractors.trap: Ships compromised work and defers heavy rework.

distractors.text: Negotiate a one-week timebox extension with the product owner so the team can address the architectural flaw and still deliver all five features.
distractors.trap: Extending the timebox breaks timeboxing discipline.

distractors.text: Ask QA to reduce their testing scope on the three affected features so the development team gains time to implement the proper fix.
distractors.trap: Cutting test coverage sacrifices quality in the timebox.

distractors.text: Cancel the review and keep coding until all five features meet the original commitment.
distractors.trap: Ignores the timebox end and stakeholder cadence.

distractors.text: Drop all five features from the review and present only the architectural redesign plan.
distractors.trap: Fails to deliver incremental value within the timebox.

distractors.text: Force overtime so the team completes a full proper fix for every affected feature this timebox.
distractors.trap: Unsustainable pressure and may still miss the limit.

explanation: Delivery performance domain and Quality: with time fixed, flex scope while building quality in.

prompts: On a data center migration, Activity K has Early Start day 25, Early Finish day 33, Late Start day 29, and Late Finish day 37. Your team lead asks whether K can be delayed to accommodate a training session on days 26-28. What should you advise?

correct.text: Activity K can be delayed up to its 4 days of total float before affecting the critical path.
correct.why: Total float equals Late Start minus Early Start (29 - 25 = 4), so the delay fits.

distractors.text: It can shift 3 days only if successors are also moved by adjusting dependencies.
distractors.trap: 3 days fits within float; no successor changes needed

distractors.text: It should start on day 29 since the Late Start is the optimal start point.
distractors.trap: Late Start consumes all float, not 'optimal'

distractors.text: It can be delayed up to 8 days, from Late Finish minus Early Start.
distractors.trap: Wrong formula; that is not float

explanation: Schedule/Delivery performance domain: total float is the delay allowed without impacting the end date.

prompts: Implementing a warehouse management system, the scheduler finds Activity T (testing) has Early Start 35, Early Finish 48, Late Start 40, Late Finish 53. Activity U (user training) follows T with an Early Start of 52. How much Free Float does Activity T have before it delays U's start?

correct.text: 4 days: successor's Early Start (52) minus Activity T's Early Finish (48).
correct.why: Free float is the delay allowed without pushing the successor's early start.

distractors.text: 5 days: Late Start (40) minus Early Start (35).
distractors.trap: That is total float, not free float

distractors.text: 13 days: Late Finish (53) minus Late Start (40) plus a buffer.
distractors.trap: Not a valid free-float formula

distractors.text: 8 days: total float added to a Late Finish/successor difference.
distractors.trap: Invented formula with no basis

explanation: Schedule/Delivery performance domain: free float is successor Early Start minus current Early Finish.

prompts: Reviewing your Gantt chart, you find Task B is scheduled to finish before its predecessor, Task A, because of an incorrect dependency setting. What should you do to correct it?
On the project Gantt, Task B is set to complete before predecessor Task A due to a bad dependency. How should you fix the schedule logic?
A schedule review shows Task B finishing ahead of its predecessor Task A because the dependency was defined incorrectly. What is the right correction?
Your Gantt has Task B ending before Task A, even though A is the predecessor, after a dependency misconfiguration. What should you do?

correct.text: Fix the dependency so Task A finishes before Task B starts.
correct.why: Correcting the logical relationship lets the schedule recalculate.

correct.text: Correct the link so Task A completes before Task B begins.
correct.why: Restoring proper predecessor logic drives valid dates.

correct.text: Update the dependency so predecessor Task A ends before successor Task B starts.
correct.why: Right dependency definition yields a coherent schedule.

distractors.text: Manually change the start and finish dates of both tasks.
distractors.trap: Treats the symptom, not the wrong logic.

distractors.text: Add resources to Task B so it finishes before Task A starts.
distractors.trap: Ignores the required sequence of work.

distractors.text: Let Task B start before Task A to speed completion.
distractors.trap: Disregards the logical dependency and adds risk.

distractors.text: Delete both tasks and re-enter them without links.
distractors.trap: Loses structure instead of fixing the relationship.

distractors.text: Mark Task B as complete early to clear the conflict visually.
distractors.trap: Falsifies progress rather than fixing logic.

distractors.text: Add lag so both tasks can finish on the same day.
distractors.trap: Does not restore the required predecessor order.

explanation: Schedule/Delivery performance domain: model correct logical dependencies rather than manipulating dates.

prompts: During planning for a two-week iteration, the team set a four-hour timebox. At three hours fifty minutes, two complex backlog items remain unestimated. The product owner says the estimates are crucial for tomorrow's forecast and wants to keep going. Committed to timeboxing, what should you do?

Sprint planning is timeboxed to four hours for a two-week iteration. With ten minutes left, two hard backlog items still lack estimates. The product owner urges continuing for tomorrow's forecast. What should you do to honor the timebox?
A two-week iteration's planning session has a four-hour limit. Near the end, two complex items are still unestimated; the product owner wants to extend for the forecast. How do you uphold timeboxing?
Four hours were set for planning a two-week sprint. At three hours fifty minutes, two tough items remain unsized and the product owner pushes to continue. What should you do?

correct.text: Stop at the four-hour limit and schedule a separate refinement slot to size the remaining items.
correct.why: The timebox is fixed; remaining sizing moves to later.

correct.text: End when four hours are up and book a follow-up refinement to estimate the leftover items.
correct.why: Timebox ends on schedule; unfinished estimates get a new slot.

correct.text: Close the session at the timebox limit and plan another refinement to size what remains.
correct.why: Protecting the timebox preserves cadence; work continues later.

distractors.text: Extend the meeting until every item is estimated for tomorrow's planning.
distractors.trap: Extending violates the fixed timebox.

distractors.text: Take a vote and, if most agree, keep going and adjust later.
distractors.trap: Group consent does not override the timebox.

distractors.text: Split the group so a subset stays to finish estimating.
distractors.trap: Breaks collective ownership and the timebox.

distractors.text: Assign rough placeholder sizes so the forecast can proceed tonight.
distractors.trap: False precision undermines forecast quality.

distractors.text: Drop the two items from the backlog until the next release planning.
distractors.trap: Avoids sizing instead of refining separately.

distractors.text: Let the product owner estimate alone after the team leaves.
distractors.trap: Bypasses team-owned estimates and collaboration.

explanation: Delivery performance domain: timeboxes are fixed constraints that protect the empirical control.

prompts: In a project schedule, the critical path is best defined as the ____.
Which statement best defines the critical path in a project schedule?
When describing schedule network analysis, the critical path is best described as the ____.
For schedule control purposes, how is the critical path best defined?

correct.text: Sequence of tasks that determines the project's overall duration.
correct.why: The critical path has no float; delays there delay the project.

correct.text: Longest path of dependent activities that sets total project duration.
correct.why: Zero-float path governs when the project can finish.

correct.text: Chain of activities whose total duration defines the project end date.
correct.why: Any slip on this path extends overall project duration.

distractors.text: Chain of the most time-consuming tasks.
distractors.trap: Long tasks may have float and not be critical.

distractors.text: List of the highest-priority tasks.
distractors.trap: Priority is unrelated to zero float.

distractors.text: Set of tasks that must be completed first.
distractors.trap: Early tasks need not determine total duration.

distractors.text: Collection of all tasks assigned to critical resources.
distractors.trap: Resource criticality is not the schedule critical path.

distractors.text: Activities with the largest remaining effort estimates.
distractors.trap: Effort size alone does not define the path.

distractors.text: Work packages that carry the highest risk ratings.
distractors.trap: Risk rating is separate from path float analysis.

explanation: Schedule/Delivery performance domain: the critical path is the longest zero-float path setting duration.

topic: Update dependency to reflect actual work
prompts: Reviewing your Gantt chart, you see 'System Configuration' (Task K) has a finish-to-finish dependency with 'Data Migration' (Task L), but K's bar extends three days past L's finish. The team lead confirms configuration genuinely needs those extra days. What is the most appropriate action?
On the Gantt, System Configuration (K) is finish-to-finish with Data Migration (L), yet K runs three days beyond L. The lead confirms those days are truly required. What should you do?
Task K (System Configuration) is linked finish-to-finish to Task L (Data Migration), but K still needs three days after L ends. The team lead affirms that need is real. What is the best action?

Your schedule shows finish-to-finish between System Configuration and Data Migration, with configuration lasting three days past migration. The lead says that extra work is genuine. How should you respond?

correct.text: Review and update the dependency type to reflect the real relationship, then assess downstream impacts.
correct.why: The schedule should model actual work logic, not force wrong links.

correct.text: Adjust the dependency to match how the work truly relates, then evaluate schedule impacts.
correct.why: Logic must mirror reality; then check effects on successors.

correct.text: Correct the link so it represents the real dependency and review effects downstream.
correct.why: Accurate relationships keep forecasts valid after the change.

distractors.text: Add resources to Task K to compress it three days and finish together.
distractors.trap: Crashes work the lead says is genuinely needed.

distractors.text: Convert to start-to-start and adjust both durations proportionally.
distractors.trap: Arbitrary change not based on work logic.

distractors.text: Extend Task L by three days to keep the finish-to-finish tie.
distractors.trap: Pads a task to satisfy a wrong dependency.

distractors.text: Hide Task K's overrun with a manual date lock on both bars.
distractors.trap: Masks the problem without correcting logic.

distractors.text: Remove Task K from the critical reports until migration ends.
distractors.trap: Hides reality instead of modeling true work.

distractors.text: Leave the chart as-is and note the mismatch only in meeting minutes.
distractors.trap: Informal notes do not fix the schedule model.

explanation: Schedule/Delivery performance domain: the schedule must reflect reality through correct dependency logic.

prompts: Building a schedule for a mobile app launch: end-user validation testing needs the backend API partially complete, frontend development can start once UI/UX designs are final, and app store submission needs both legal's privacy-policy review and marketing's app description. The sponsor asks which activities can run in parallel. Which statement is accurate?

For a mobile app release schedule, validation testing waits on a partially ready backend API, frontend can begin when UI/UX designs are final, and store submission needs legal privacy review plus marketing's description. Which activities can truly run in parallel?

A consumer app launch plan shows: user validation depends on a partial backend API; frontend starts after UI/UX is final; store submission needs legal and marketing inputs. Which statement about parallel work is correct?

Scheduling a mobile launch: validation testing needs backend API partially done; frontend starts once UI/UX is finalized; store submission needs legal privacy review and marketing copy. Which parallel-work statement is accurate?

correct.text: Backend API and frontend development can overlap, since they have different predecessors and can proceed after their own triggers.
correct.why: Independent predecessors let the two activities run concurrently.

correct.text: Backend API work and frontend development may run together because each starts from its own independent predecessor.
correct.why: Separate triggers allow concurrent progress and a shorter timeline.

correct.text: Frontend development and backend API work can proceed in parallel once each meets its own start condition.
correct.why: Different predecessors enable overlap without false sequencing.

distractors.text: User validation testing and app store submission can run in parallel as both depend on upstream deliverables.
distractors.trap: They sit at different points and do not overlap.

distractors.text: Frontend must wait for backend completion because UI/UX depends on the architecture.
distractors.trap: Frontend only needs finalized UI/UX designs.

distractors.text: Legal review and marketing should be sequenced consecutively to coordinate before submission.
distractors.trap: They are independent and can run in parallel.

distractors.text: All launch activities must be fully sequential to protect quality gates.
distractors.trap: Over-serializes work that can safely overlap.

distractors.text: App store submission can start as soon as frontend development begins.
distractors.trap: Submission needs legal and marketing, not just frontend start.

distractors.text: Validation testing can begin immediately with no backend progress required.
distractors.trap: Validation needs the backend API at least partially complete.

explanation: Schedule/Delivery performance domain: activities with independent predecessors can run concurrently.

prompts: On an infrastructure upgrade, schedule analysis gives Activity F an Early Start of day 20, Early Finish day 28, Late Start day 24, and Late Finish day 32. A subcontractor asks to start F on day 22 instead of 20 to coordinate with another project. How should you respond?

correct.text: The request is acceptable: F has 4 days of total float, so a day-22 start stays within the Late Start of day 24.
correct.why: Total float (24 - 20 = 4) allows a two-day delay without affecting completion.

distractors.text: Acceptable because F has 8 days of free float allowing a 2-day shift.
distractors.trap: 8 days of float is miscalculated

distractors.text: Decline it, since any deviation from Early Start delays the project.
distractors.trap: Positive float absorbs delay without impact

distractors.text: It needs Monte Carlo simulation before deciding.
distractors.trap: Overkill for a simple float calculation

explanation: Schedule/Delivery performance domain: total float permits a delayed start up to the Late Start.

prompts: You are reviewing the network diagram for a product development effort. The testing phase has a finish-to-finish relationship with the documentation phase, and the documentation team says it needs the final test results before it can wrap up. What does this relationship tell you about how the two activities are scheduled?
In a product network diagram, testing and documentation share a finish-to-finish link; documentation needs final test results to close. What does that relationship mean for scheduling?
Testing is finish-to-finish with documentation on your product schedule, and docs cannot finish without final test results. How should you interpret that relationship?
A network review shows documentation finish-to-finish with testing because final results are needed to complete the docs. What does this relationship indicate?

correct.text: Documentation cannot finish until testing finishes; the two may run in parallel but their completions are linked.
correct.why: Finish-to-finish ties successor finish to predecessor finish, not starts.

correct.text: Docs may proceed alongside testing, but documentation cannot complete until testing is complete.
correct.why: FF links finishes so both can overlap until linked ends.

correct.text: The phases can overlap, yet documentation's finish waits on testing's finish.
correct.why: A finish-to-finish constraint couples completion dates only.

distractors.text: Testing must be fully complete before documentation can begin.
distractors.trap: Describes finish-to-start, not finish-to-finish.

distractors.text: The two must start together because they share resources.
distractors.trap: FF links finishes, not starts or resources.

distractors.text: Documentation's finish date controls when testing must finish.
distractors.trap: Reverses the dependency direction.

distractors.text: Neither activity may start until both teams are fully staffed.
distractors.trap: Staffing is unrelated to finish-to-finish logic.

distractors.text: Testing duration must equal documentation duration exactly.
distractors.trap: FF does not force equal activity lengths.

distractors.text: Documentation automatically inherits testing's start date.
distractors.trap: FF does not dictate successor start timing.

explanation: Tests sequencing logic within the Planning performance domain: a finish-to-finish dependency links activity completions, not their start dates.

prompts: Midway through a project you apply fast tracking, and your next status report shows a positive schedule variance. What does this indicate?
After mid-project fast tracking, the status report reports a positive schedule variance. What does that mean?
You used fast tracking partway through delivery, and earned-value results now show positive schedule variance. What does this indicate?
Halfway into execution, following a fast-track decision, schedule variance is positive on the status report. How should you interpret it?

correct.text: The project is ahead of schedule.
correct.why: Positive SV (EV − PV) means earned work exceeds planned work.

correct.text: Work is progressing faster than the baseline plan.
correct.why: Positive schedule variance signals performance ahead of plan.

correct.text: Schedule performance is better than planned to date.
correct.why: SV > 0 indicates the project is ahead of schedule.

distractors.text: The project is behind schedule.
distractors.trap: A negative SV, not positive, signals a slip.

distractors.text: The project is over budget.
distractors.trap: Budget status is shown by cost variance.

distractors.text: The project is exactly on schedule.
distractors.trap: On-schedule is SV = 0, not positive.

distractors.text: The project has exhausted its contingency reserve.
distractors.trap: Reserves are not measured by schedule variance.

distractors.text: The critical path has automatically lengthened.
distractors.trap: Positive SV does not imply a longer path.

distractors.text: Scope has increased without a change request.
distractors.trap: SV measures schedule, not unauthorized scope growth.

explanation: Measurement performance domain: schedule variance is an earned value metric where a positive value means ahead of plan.

prompts: Your Gantt chart defines a start-to-start relationship between Task G (predecessor) and Task H (successor), but Task H is currently scheduled to start before Task G. Absent any resource or other constraints, what is the best course of action?
A start-to-start link ties predecessor Task G to successor Task H, yet H is set to start before G. With no resource constraints, what should you do?
On the Gantt, Tasks G and H are start-to-start (G predecessor), but H begins earlier than G. No other constraints apply. What is the best fix?
Schedule logic shows start-to-start from Task G to Task H, while H is planned to start first. Ignoring resource limits, how should you correct this?

correct.text: Reschedule so Task G starts before or at the same time as Task H.
correct.why: In start-to-start, the successor cannot start before the predecessor.

correct.text: Adjust dates so predecessor G starts no later than successor H starts.
correct.why: SS requires the predecessor to start first or together.

correct.text: Move the starts so G begins before or concurrent with H.
correct.why: Start-to-start forbids the successor starting earlier alone.

distractors.text: Leave Task H starting first and shift Task G to follow it.
distractors.trap: Violates the start-to-start logic.

distractors.text: Start both tasks together and accept the mismatch.
distractors.trap: Ignores that H currently precedes G.

distractors.text: Swap the labels so H becomes the predecessor G.
distractors.trap: Renaming changes the defined sequence arbitrarily.

distractors.text: Convert the link to finish-to-start without reviewing the work.
distractors.trap: Changes type instead of enforcing defined SS logic.

distractors.text: Add lag so H can keep starting earlier than G.
distractors.trap: Lag does not justify reversing SS start order.

distractors.text: Shorten Task H so the early start no longer matters.
distractors.trap: Duration change does not fix start-order violation.

explanation: Planning performance domain: a start-to-start dependency requires the predecessor to start no later than the successor.

prompts: On a data center relocation schedule, three dependencies are flagged: server decommissioning before physical transport, cable management after rack installation, and firewall configuration after network testing (per the security team's standard procedures). The sponsor wants to cut five days. Which dependency offers the least flexibility for schedule adjustment?

A data center move lists: decommission servers before transport; manage cables after racks go in; configure firewalls after network testing per security procedures. The sponsor seeks five days of compression. Which dependency is least flexible?

Relocating a data center, you track three links: servers must be decommissioned before transport; cabling follows rack install by preference; firewall work follows network tests per security standards. To cut five days, which dependency is least flexible?

For a data center relocation, dependencies include mandatory decommission-before-transport, preference-based cable work after racks, and procedure-based firewall config after network testing. Which offers the least schedule flexibility when cutting five days?

correct.text: Server decommissioning before physical transport, a mandatory dependency inherent to the work.
correct.why: Mandatory hard logic reflects physical necessity and resists reordering.

correct.text: Decommissioning servers before transport—the hard, inherent dependency in the work.
correct.why: Mandatory dependencies cannot be reordered to compress the schedule.

correct.text: The decommission-before-transport link, because it is a mandatory physical constraint.
correct.why: Hard dependencies are the least flexible for schedule compression.

distractors.text: Firewall configuration after network testing, set by security procedures.
distractors.trap: Procedure-based sequencing is discretionary and negotiable.

distractors.text: Cable management after rack installation, based on team preference.
distractors.trap: Preference-based sequencing is discretionary, not fixed.

distractors.text: All three are equally flexible since they were documented together.
distractors.trap: When documented does not change dependency type.

distractors.text: None of them can change because the sponsor requested a cut.
distractors.trap: Sponsor pressure does not make all links mandatory.

distractors.text: Only external vendor lead times, which are not among the three listed.
distractors.trap: Question asks among the three flagged dependencies.

distractors.text: Firewall work, because security rules always override physical constraints.
distractors.trap: Policy is often discretionary; physical necessity is hard.
